author: TakemiSora
body:
### Problem description

Attempting to press Ctrl and/or Alt with PgUp/PgDown does not send the correct keys to the shell.

In the video, the other app besides termux is AVNC client connected to vncserver from termux. Where you can see that pressing ctrl+pgup makes `^[[5;5~` appear where as in termux it just sends the PgUp.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bb016b9c-cafb-4d69-8fa1-167c9674bac3

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

- Open Termux App
- Use `showkey -a` or `cat -v`
- Attempt to press Ctrl and then PgUp (and also just PgUp)
- Both will return the same output

### What is the expected behavior?

The correct keys should be sent.

### System information

* Termux application version: 0.118.3
* Android OS version: 16
* Device model: Motorola Edge 50 Fusion
title: [Bug]: Ctrl+PgUp Not registering

author: GG-Feng
body:
### Feature description

Hi everyone,

I would like to share an idea about doing some compatibility validation work around possible future JDK baseline upgrades for `termux-app`.

This idea is not coming out of nowhere. Looking at the current project setup, `termux-app` already uses JDK 17 in its CI/build workflow, and the project has previously handled Gradle build compatibility issues related to JDK 17. At the same time, the Android code still keeps its source/target compatibility at Java 8.

Because of that, I think it is possible that a baseline upgrade may eventually become part of future maintenance work. If so, it may be useful to prepare for possible future JDK baseline upgrades in advance, instead of only dealing with migration issues when an upgrade becomes necessary. This could help make future maintenance work smoother and more efficient.

To be clear, this proposal is not asking the upstream project to change its official Java source baseline right now. It is also not asking the project to maintain multiple codebases, create Java-specific official releases, replace the existing test suite, or maintain a performance-focused fork.

My goal is to do early compatibility validation against newer build JDK environments and possible future baseline changes. For example, this could include testing with JDK 17, JDK 21, or later LTS releases, while still respecting the fact that the current source/target compatibility may remain unchanged unless the project decides otherwise.

If the project later considers moving its build setup, toolchain, or parts of its source baseline to a newer JDK, this work may help surface issues earlier, such as:

* Build or compilation failures
* Test failures
* Runtime behavior differences
* Dependency or Gradle / Android Gradle Plugin compatibility issues
* CI and environment setup problems
* Issues that users or contributors may encounter when building with newer Java runtimes

My current idea is to maintain a small number of experimental compatibility branches externally. The upstream main branch can continue normal development based on the current official setup, while we take responsibility for syncing, testing, and maintaining those experimental branches.

These branches are not intended to become official separate codebases unless the project and community later find clear value in them. At this stage, their main purpose is to validate compatibility, collect feedback, and prepare useful migration reference information.

If there is sustained community interest, we may maintain two or three external compatibility branches targeting newer JDK environments over the long term and report useful findings back upstream. When appropriate, we would submit small, focused PRs for concrete compatibility issues instead of asking the project to review or maintain a large fork.

The goal of this early-stage idea is to reduce future migration risk and provide useful compatibility information without adding extra maintenance burden to the official project.

Thanks for your time, and thanks for maintaining Termux.


### Additional information

empty
title: External Compatibility Testing for Newer Build JDKs in termux-app

author: Agg-dev
body:
### Feature description

Currently, the extra-keys row has an `popup` feature. While this is good, it's not ergonomic compared to a flick down, because the extra keys row sits **ABOVE** the keyboard, meaning you have to reach your hand higher to first touch the extra keys row, and even higher to swipe up.
A simple flickdown would allow users to simply flick down without streching thumbs.

### Additional information

Other terminal emulators don't have support for it, as they're not designed for phones.
Only thing I can think of is GBoard and Unexpected keyboard, as you can do something like this there.
title: [Feature]: Flick down on extra-keys row

author: hanslub42
body:
### Feature description


Add parameter `--display` to `am` (e.g. `am start --display 9 no.nrk.yr/.MainActivity`)

### Additional information

Since version 4.0 `scrcpy` allows opening a new display  (`scrcpy --new-display=.....`). Starting termux via `scrpy` (`scrcpy --new-display=1000x1000  --start-app=com.termux`) works well, `termux` will open on the new display. However, when I then use this `termux` instance to start an app (say with `$ am start no.nrk.yr/.MainActivity`) the app will appear on display 0 (the phone itself), and not on the new display, as I expected.

`am --help` does not mention displays at all. Is there a workaround?
title: [Feature]: Add parameter --display to  `am start`

author: Sushan64
body: ### Problem description Running on Android 15 with Termux. Trying to install: ``` pip install google-genai ``` Fails because `cryptography` package tries to build from source using `maturin` + Rust, but Rust standard library crates (`std`, `core`, `alloc`) are not found in rlib format on Termux. **Error:** ``` error: crate `std` required to be available in rlib format, but was not found in this form error: crate `core` required to be available in rlib format, but was not found in this form error: could not compile `proc-macro2` due to 19 previous errors ``` ### Steps to reproduce the behavior. You should have python and pip Then, pip install google-genai Or pip install cryptography ### What is the expected behavior? ERROR: Failed to build 'cryptography' when installing build dependencies for cryptography ### System information * Termux application version: 0.118.3 * Android OS version: 15 * Device model: 23021RAAEG / Redmi
title: [Bug]: google-genai installation fails on Termux

# new config file 

enable 30 minute time out with a different config file 

~~~
vim $DATA/shared_prefs/config.xml
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='utf-8' standalone='yes' ?>
<map>
	<int name="idle_timeout" value="30" />
</map>
~~~

if you try to edit this file 

~~~
vim $DATA/shared_prefs/com.termux_preferences.xml
~~~

the changes will be overridden by commit functions designed for GUI with absolutely no CLI in mind

the new config file is CLI aware. it will not be changed randomly by commit functions 


# keep or close   orphans 


my termux session will restore an existing tmux session  

~~~
vim .bashrc
procs=$(pidof tmux | wc | tr -s " " | cut -f3 -d" ")
if [ $procs -eq 0 ]; then
	tmux
elif test $procs -eq 1; then
	test -z "$TMUX" && tmux a
fi
~~~

one time this tmux session was still running seven hours later 

~~~
logcat -d|ack -i alarm|tail
05-23 23:00:00.431 26893 26893 D Termux.SystemEventReceiver:   ALARM shut down  battery level   93%

date|copy
Sun May 24 06:35:54 ADT 2026
termux-battery-status | jq .level
92
~~~

battery drain was negligible it was as probably frozen and did no harm

I don't know if it happens when termux is in the foreground or why it doesn't purge child apps . usually they are quickly removed when termux is gone  

I have tried to remove them myself to save battery 

john-peterson/termux-app@orphan

but I reverted back to keeping them because I some times accidentally cross the 32 app limit and lose the tmux client and crucially the only termux bash child

~~~
pstree

which will close termux

but if I quickly start it again the server is some times still running with my entire session intact 

this has led me to consider a redesigne to ask if I want to restart bash instead of closing termux with the hope of saving my tmux server and session from disappearing

if you trip the 32 app limit the first child app will be closed by android . this will also close the tmux client and perhaps incorrectly the entire termux session and service  

~~~
ack -i signal -t java
terminal-emulator/src/main/java/com/termux/terminal/TerminalSession.java
exitDescription += " (signal " + (-exitCode) + ")";
exitDescription += " - press Enter]";
~~~

this could be re designed to restart the bash client instead of closing termux   and risk losing all children 

~~~

~~~

this might save the tmux service and all children 

when I close termux is seems like the system immediately close all children . or even termux I don't know what it does when closing exactly

~~~
gh search code TYPE_APP_POWER_INTENSIVE
jingpad-bsp/vendor_sprd_platform_packages_apps_Settings src/com/android/settings/fuelgauge/SprdPowerIntensiveAppsPreferenceController.java
~~~

it's in a fragment that can't be launched from terminal without adb

I am impressed by the idle drain on my unisoc phones but they haven't been able to convince upstream apparently 



# todo same for termux API 

termux API should also be removed from the list with the same fix 

the listening socket should time out when screen off for a long time 

a sad update in my android 14 unisoc the API socket connection fails constantly . otherwise everything works the same as in android 13 



# race between alarm and cancel alarm 

you may see termux close when you unlock your phone . this is not a coincidence it's a race condition 

there is no elegant way to avoid it

my investigation of all alternative  is in

john-peterson/openapp@timer

all the more complicated solutions are riddled with bugs . this solution is the simplest and best it never fails


# my branch versus this branch 

if the build fails it's because I use another  branch with all my patches 

john-peterson/termux-app@alarm

barring  possible  typos this is a solid patch I have used it for months it never fails


# idle drain problem  

the only way to get idle drain to zero is to remove or patch all apps on the power intensive apps list . and use battery saver	

with out battery saver	idle drain is so severe battery only last three days .
 even when all radios are off with air plane mode active  

battery saver  makes a gigantic difference around a factor of ten 

if	 battery saver	is functional battery drain should be three per cent per day or  equivalent one month 

on expedition this should make it possible	 to retrieve a location occasionally   for a month until a broken sun panel can be replaced 

I was is Australia when this problem became so severe I had to create this patch 

the crucial setting for this drastic difference is 

~~~
force_background_check
~~~

these have only minor effect on long term drain 

~~~
force_all_apps_standby
enable_quick_doze
~~~

and increase drain for wake up. it requires more battery to return from a deep sleep state . the others are meaningless and

~~~
enable_night_mode
~~~

a disaster because the phone can't be used in day light 

I have posted a new settings API that solve this problem as explained on the post 

there is no way to reduce idle drain more or completely block back ground workers for most apps . this is a job opportunity 

some app stores are notorious for their enormous bombardment of meaningless workers to increase ad revenue and engagement . but this applies to many apps with aggressive monetisation or poor design
title: [Patch] | PR BLOCKED . posting patch as issue instead | idle timeout : idle drain safe guard . remove from power intensive apps

Hello Termux Team,

I am writing to report a critical issue regarding the repository mirrors and environment stability on non-rooted Android devices.

Recently, running standard package updates (pkg update && pkg upgrade) consistently throws '404 Not Found' errors across multiple core mirrors. Furthermore, attempting to manage external distributions via proot-distro results in sudden terminal crashes and broken loop states. As an end-user, these issues completely break the environment.

Please provide updated and working rootfs installation commands/scripts for external distros that do not throw 404 errors, and fix the core stability bugs on Android.

pkg update && pkg upgrade
proot-distro install parrot

The environment should update smoothly without throwing '404 Not Found' errors, and external Linux distributions should install cleanly via rootfs scripts without crashing the terminal.

* Termux application version:
* Android OS version:
* Device model:

### Problem description

Pasting the ASCII control character ESC (\x1B, U+001B) alone into the Termux terminal session (via the paste action from the context menu) causes an IllegalArgumentException: length <= 0 in ByteQueue.write(), immediately crashing the application.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8375465d-5c26-4c17-be29-09f5d7215558

1. Copy the single character ESC (\x1B, U+001B) to the clipboard.
2. Open Termux on Android.
3. Long-press in the terminal area to open the context menu.
4. Tap Paste.

author: davidj4tech
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem?

The extras row has a useful "text input box" mode (accessed by swiping the row horizontally) that lets the active IME submit text in batch ΓÇö essential for voice typing, autocomplete, and other IMEs that don't play well with raw keystrokes into the terminal.

The mode is currently only reachable via the horizontal swipe gesture. When using Termux with a hardware keyboard (e.g. a Bluetooth keyboard or a tablet/foldable in keyboard mode), reaching up to touch the screen to swipe the extras row breaks the flow.

Related: #82 (the original 2016 ask was for "a textarea input mode which can be toggled with **a key**"; it was closed when the textarea was implemented, but only as a swipe gesture ΓÇö the *key* part of that original request was never delivered).

### Describe the solution you'd like

Expose a configurable HW-keyboard shortcut in `termux.properties`, alongside the existing four:

```
# shortcut.create-session = ctrl + t
# shortcut.next-session = ctrl + 2
# shortcut.previous-session = ctrl + 1
# shortcut.rename-session = ctrl + n
# shortcut.toggle-input-mode = ctrl + i   # NEW
```

Pressing the configured chord would trigger the same handler the left-swipe currently does (swap extras row Γåö input field), and a second press (or a different shortcut) returns to the extras row.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

- Automate / Tasker with AccessibilityService gestures simulating the swipe at fixed coordinates. Works, but is fragile (coordinates are device-specific, requires another app, needs accessibility permission).
- `su -c "input swipe ..."` (requires root, also coordinate-fragile).

A first-class shortcut is dramatically simpler and lines up with the existing extensibility model of `termux.properties`.

### Additional context

Implementation hint: the swipe is currently handled in `TerminalView` / `TermuxActivity`. The shortcut would presumably wire into `TermuxActivity.onKeyDown` next to the existing `shortcut.*` handlers and call the same `toggleInputMode` (or equivalent) method.
title: Add shortcut.toggle-input-mode (HW keyboard shortcut for extras-row Γçä text-input swipe)

author: venkyqz
body:
## Environment

- App: Termux (`com.termux`)
- Branch checked: `master`
- File: `app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/api/file/FileReceiverActivity.java`:144
- Verified commit: `30ebb2dee381d292ade0f2868cfde0f9f20b89fe`

### The Issue

While reviewing `FileReceiverActivity.handleContentUri() ΓåÆ ContentResolver-query`, I noticed a potential main-thread blocking risk around `android.content.ContentResolver#query(android.net.Uri,java.lang.String[],java.lang.String,java.lang.String[],java.lang.String)`.

The latest source still operates synchronously:

```java
141:             String attachmentFileName = null;
142: 
143:             String[] projection = new String[]{OpenableColumns.DISPLAY_NAME};
144:             try (Cursor c = getContentResolver().query(uri, projection, null, null, null)) {
145:                 if (c != null && c.moveToFirst()) {
146:                     final int fileNameColumnId = c.getColumnIndex(OpenableColumns.DISPLAY_NAME);
147:                     if (fileNameColumnId >= 0) attachmentFileName = c.getString(fileNameColumnId);
148:                 }
149:             }
```

`android.content.ContentResolver#query(android.net.Uri,java.lang.String[],java.lang.String,java.lang.String[],java.lang.String)` can block the calling thread while Android resolves a provider, opens a stream, performs database work, contacts account services, touches storage, or waits on remote I/O, depending on the API and URI/backend involved.

### The Risk & Impact

If this method is reached from a UI-thread path, the operation can cause visible jank, StrictMode violations in debug builds, or an ANR when the provider/backend is slow, blocked, or unreachable. I am phrasing this as a source-level risk because I verified the current source pattern but did not run an on-device reproduction.

### Current source path

```text
<com.termux.app.api.file.FileReceiverActivity: void handleContentUri(android.net.Uri,java.lang.String)>
  -> android.content.ContentResolver#query(android.net.Uri,java.lang.String[],java.lang.String,java.lang.String[],java.lang.String)
```

### Verification

I checked the latest upstream source at `30ebb2dee381d292ade0f2868cfde0f9f20b89fe` and found the sensitive operation still present in `app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/api/file/FileReceiverActivity.java`. This report is based on source-level inspection, not runtime reproduction.
title: [Bug]: Potential main-thread blocking risk

author: venkyqz
body:
## Environment

- App: Termux (`com.termux`)
- Branch checked: `master`
- File: `app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/api/file/FileReceiverActivity.java`:154

### The Issue

While reviewing `FileReceiverActivity.handleContentUri() ΓåÆ ContentResolver-openInputStream`, I noticed a potential main-thread blocking risk around `android.content.ContentResolver#openInputStream(android.net.Uri)`.

The latest source still operates synchronously:

```java
151:             if (attachmentFileName == null) attachmentFileName = subjectFromIntent;

158:             Logger.logStackTraceWithMessage(LOG_TAG, "handleContentUri(uri=" + uri + ") failed", e);

```

`android.content.ContentResolver#openInputStream(android.net.Uri)` can block the calling thread while Android resolves a provider, opens a stream, performs database work, contacts account services, touches storage, or waits on remote I/O, depending on the API and URI/backend involved.

### The Risk & Impact

If this method is reached from a UI-thread path, the operation can cause visible jank, StrictMode violations in debug builds, or an ANR when the provider/backend is slow, blocked, or unreachable. I am phrasing this as a source-level risk because I verified the current source pattern but did not run an on-device reproduction.

### Current source path

```text
<com.termux.app.api.file.FileReceiverActivity: void handleContentUri(android.net.Uri,java.lang.String)>
  -> android.content.ContentResolver#openInputStream(android.net.Uri)
```


### Verification

I checked the latest upstream source at `30ebb2dee381d292ade0f2868cfde0f9f20b89fe` and found the sensitive operation still present in `app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/api/file/FileReceiverActivity.java`. This report is based on source-level inspection, not runtime reproduction.

author: hansm629
body:
### Problem description

https://github.com/termux/termux-app/issues/5035

I have identified the cause of the issue regarding the above issue, where the **Big Core Cluster** is not being used on **Samsung OneUI 8.x devices**.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

The issue was that when the Termux App is in the background,
even though the battery usage limit is set to [**Unrestricted**],

**it does not use the Big Core Cluster at all, resulting in performance degradation.**

To my knowledge, the **Termux App** already has the `android.permission.FOREGROUND_SERVICE` permission declared in `AndroidManifest.xml`, and when tested with `adb`, the **Termux App** is recognized as a **Foreground Service app**.

This appears to be a common issue across **OneUI 8.x**, and it is presumed to be an issue where the Big Core Cluster is not being used despite the battery usage limit being set to [Unrestricted], likely due to a shift in the CPU workload policy to be more conservative.

I have also been reporting this issue on the Samsung Developer Forum...
(https://forum.developer.samsung.com/t/detail?id=10128)

Is there any way to mitigate this issue at the Termux App level?

Thank you.

### What is the expected behavior?

_No response_

### System information

- Termux application version: Termux 0.119.0-beta.3
- Android OS version: One UI 8.x(Android 16) / One UI 7.0(Android 15)
- Device model: Galaxy S24+ / Galaxy S25 Ultra / Galaxy S26 Ultra / Galaxy Tab S9 Ultra
title: [Bug]: Samsung OneUI 8.x: Performance issue with Termux app in the background

author: xJonathanLEI
body:
### Feature description

Currently, the only ways to toggle the visibility of the terminal toolbar appear to just be:

- Long-pressing the keyboard button
- Volume + `q`
- Volume + `k`

all of which involve physically operating the device. There also seems to be no way to programmatically toggle this setting.

As a long time Termux user I find myself using the app in two modes:

1. Handheld mode: using the toolbar + virtual keyboard
2. Desktop mode: using the device only as a screen; w/ toolbar and virtual keyboard disabled - use external physical keyboard

When switching from mode 1 to mode 2 by turning on an external keyboard, most systems are smart enough to automatically hide the virtual keyboard. But the terminal toolbar itself remains visible. On small devices every pixel counts and the toolbar takes up valuable screen real estate. So I'd end up having to reach for the phone again to turn it off.

A simple fix would be to just allow programmatic access to toggling the terminal toolbar so that it can be set as an alias in the shell.

### Additional information

N/A
title: [Feature]: Make terminal toolbar toggle scriptable

author: sandy2008
body:
### Problem description

On Android with an external **JIS/Japanese hardware keyboard** (in our case a Xiaomi keyboard), ordinary English typing works in Termux, but **JIS-specific hardware keys do not behave usefully**.

The main keys of interest are:

- `σìèΦºÆ/σà¿ΦºÆ` / `Zenkaku/Hankaku`
- `Henkan`
- `Muhenkan`
- `Yen`
- `Ro`

This is related to older reports about external language switching and hardware keyboard mapping, but the current problem is narrower: Termux appears to handle only a subset of Android keyboard-layout/language-switch keys from external keyboards.

### Current understanding

Looking at current `master`, `TerminalView.onKeyDown()` already has a special case for `KEYCODE_LANGUAGE_SWITCH`, which was likely added to address external keyboard language switching cases such as #4133.

However, I could not find corresponding handling for common JIS-specific Android keycodes such as:

- `KEYCODE_ZENKAKU_HANKAKU`
- `KEYCODE_HENKAN`
- `KEYCODE_MUHENKAN`
- `KEYCODE_YEN`
- `KEYCODE_RO`

So the present behavior seems to be:

- ordinary characters work
- standard terminal keycodes work
- `KEYCODE_LANGUAGE_SWITCH` is passed through
- JIS-only hardware keys are still not supported or not translated in a useful way

### Why this matters

On Android, these keys are important for real-world external Japanese keyboards.

For terminal usage, there are two practical classes of behavior:

1. **IME/language toggle keys** such as `Zenkaku/Hankaku`, `Henkan`, `Muhenkan`
2. **JIS-specific symbol keys** such as `Yen` and `Ro`

I am **not** asking Termux to implement a full IME framework like IBus/Fcitx.

I am asking whether Termux can either:

- clearly document that these JIS hardware keys are intentionally unsupported, or
- support a minimal handling strategy for them, for example:
  - passing the IME/language-toggle keys through in the same spirit as `KEYCODE_LANGUAGE_SWITCH`
  - and/or optionally mapping JIS-specific symbol keys to terminal-friendly output

### Related issues

- #137 `halfwidth/fullwidth key doesn't work since 0.38`
- #1839 `Typing in Korean with an external keyboard`
- #1210 `Hardware Keyboard Mapping`
- #4133 `[Bug]: Cannot change language with external keyboard.`

From #4133, there was even a local patch posted that added explicit handling for `KEYCODE_LANGUAGE_SWITCH`, and current `master` already seems to include that path.

### Questions

1. Is the lack of handling for JIS-specific hardware keys intentional?
2. Would a narrow patch be acceptable for the Android keycodes above, without introducing a broad Android-only virtual-key abstraction?
3. If not, what is the recommended workaround for external JIS keyboards on Android in Termux?

### Additional context

This seems adjacent to, but not identical with, generic system-wide hardware keyboard layout problems. For example, #4826 was closed as effectively system-wide keyboard mapping, while this issue is about **Termux's current handling of Android JIS-specific keycodes from external keyboards**.
title: JIS external keyboard keys (YEN/RO/HENKAN/MUHENKAN/ZENKAKU_HANKAKU) are still not handled in current Termux

author: stevenxxiu
body:
### Feature description

Support the *Kitty* image protocol.

#2973 implements the *Sixel* image protocol, but this is a rather legacy protocol. Many programs now only support the *Kitty* image protocol to show images. Terminal emulators too, like *Ghostty*.

### Additional information

Per <https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/graphics-protocol/>, terminal emulators with support include:

- [Ghostty](https://ghostty.org)
- [Konsole](https://invent.kde.org/utilities/konsole/-/merge_requests/594)
- [st (with a patch)](https://st.suckless.org/patches/kitty-graphics-protocol)
- [Warp](https://docs.warp.dev/getting-started/changelog#id-2025.03.26-v0.2025.03.26.08.10)
- [wayst](https://github.com/91861/wayst)
- [WezTerm](https://github.com/wez/wezterm/issues/986)
- [iTerm2](https://github.com/gnachman/iTerm2/commit/4fe5b2173193b6c3e45234b6b2ab7a144a5cfa01)
- [xterm.js](https://github.com/xtermjs/xterm.js/discussions/5683)
title: [Feature]: Support the Kitty image protocol to show images

author: GlowingScrewdriver
body:
### Feature description

Often, when an app shares a small snippet of text with Termux, the filename is empty. If one wishes to process such text snippets with `termux-file-editor`, one must type in some filename in order to proceed. This is a mild inconvenience, especially in cases when the text doesn't really need to be saved, and the filename is not important.

I can think of 2 alternative ways of handling the filename in these situations:

1. If the user chooses to leave the filename empty, Termux could write the file contents to a temporary file, pass that to `termux-file-editor`, and delete the temporary file. So the interaction would go something like:

    1. User shares from an app to Termux
    2. Termux's "Save file in" dialog opens up, asking for a filename
    3. User clicks "edit" without filling in a filename
    4. `termux-file-editor` receives a temporary file as its argument
    5. The temporary file is deleted after `termux-file-editor` exits

2. A fixed default filename is provided by Termux. Here, the interaction would look like:
    1. User shares from an app to Termux
    2. Termux's "Save file in" dialog opens up, asking for a filename. The filename field is already filled with the default (something like `.termux_shared`?)
    3. User clicks "edit" without changing the default filename
    4. `termux-file-editor`, as expected, simply receives this default filename as its argument

### Additional information

--
title: [Feature]: termux-file-editor: default filename

author: AKSHAY355-a
body:
### Problem description

When a user renames a Termux terminal session via long-press on the session name in the sidebar (drawer), the persistent notification shown in the Android notification shade does not reflect the new session name. The notification continues to display the old/default session name (e.g., "Termux") instead of the user-defined name.

This is a UI inconsistency between the in-app session drawer (which correctly shows the renamed session) and the persistent notification, which always shows the stale/default name. The bug persists even after switching between sessions or bringing the app to the foreground.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

1. Open Termux app (v0.118.x, installed via F-Droid or GitHub release).
2. Open the left sidebar/drawer by swiping from the left edge.
3. Long-press on the current session name (e.g., "Termux").
4. When prompted, enter a new session name (e.g., "MySession") and confirm.
5. Observe the session name in the drawer ΓÇö it correctly shows "MySession".
6. Pull down the Android notification shade.
7. Observe the Termux persistent notification ΓÇö it still shows the old session name ("Termux") instead of "MySession".
8. Switch to another session and then back to "MySession".
9. The notification still does not update to the new session name.

### What is the expected behavior?

The persistent Termux notification in the Android notification shade should immediately reflect the updated session name after the user renames a session via long-press. The notification title or session label should stay in sync with the in-app session drawer at all times.

### System information

- **Termux Version**: 0.118.3 (installed via F-Droid)
- **Android Version**: Android 13 (SDK 33)
- **Device**: Realme device (also reproduced on Samsung Galaxy running Android 13)
- **Architecture**: aarch64 (ARM64)
- **Android API Level**: 33
- **Kernel**: Linux 5.10.x (aarch64)
- **`uname -a` output**: `Linux localhost 5.10.157-android13-4 #1 SMP PREEMPT aarch64 GNU/Linux`
- **Installation source**: F-Droid (not Google Play)

**Additional context:**
- The bug is consistently reproducible.
- The `TermuxService` notification builder does not call `notificationManager.notify()` after the session title is updated in `TerminalSession.setTitle()`.
- Checked in `TermuxService.java`: the `updateNotification()` method is not triggered on session rename events from `TerminalSessionClient.onTitleChanged()`.
title: [Bug]: Terminal session notification does not update session name when renamed via long-press

author: Sayeem3051
body:
### Problem description

Several `PendingIntent` calls in the codebase use flags `0` or `FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT` without including `FLAG_IMMUTABLE`. On Android 12+ (API 31+), this throws `IllegalArgumentException` and crashes the app.

Affected files:
- [TermuxService.java](cci:7://file:///c:/Users/shaba/projects/open-source-contribute/termux/termux-app/app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/TermuxService.java:0:0-0:0) ΓÇö lines 787, 830, 838
- [TermuxPluginUtils.java](cci:7://file:///c:/Users/shaba/projects/open-source-contribute/termux/termux-app/termux-shared/src/main/java/com/termux/shared/termux/plugins/TermuxPluginUtils.java:0:0-0:0) ΓÇö lines 383, 387
- [TermuxCrashUtils.java](cci:7://file:///c:/Users/shaba/projects/open-source-contribute/termux/termux-app/termux-shared/src/main/java/com/termux/shared/termux/crash/TermuxCrashUtils.java:0:0-0:0) ΓÇö lines 345, 349

Currently masked because `targetSdkVersion=28`, but will become a hard crash when targetSdk is raised to 31+ (Google Play requires 34+).

I'm happy to submit a PR with the fix.


### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

1. Change `targetSdkVersion` to 31 or higher in [gradle.properties](cci:7://file:///c:/Users/shaba/projects/open-source-contribute/termux/termux-app/gradle.properties:0:0-0:0)
2. Build and install on an Android 12+ device
3. Open Termux ΓÇö the foreground notification PendingIntent crashes the app

Expected logcat:
java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Targeting S+ (version 31 and above) requires that one of FLAG_IMMUTABLE or FLAG_MUTABLE be specified when creating a PendingIntent.


### What is the expected behavior?

PendingIntents should include FLAG_IMMUTABLE to prevent crashes on Android 12+ and prepare for targetSdkVersion upgrade.


### System information

* Termux application version: latest from master branch
* Android OS version: Android 12+ (API 31+)
* Device model: Any (code-level issue, not device-specific)
title: [Bug]:Missing PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE causes crash on Android 12+ (API 31+)

author: hansm629
body: ### Problem description Hello, I would like to report a possible performance regression affecting Termux on **Samsung Galaxy devices** running **One UI 8.x**. Issue: On **One UI 8.x**, tested heavy native workloads launched from Termux no longer use the big CPU cluster properly. In my testing, the big-core cluster does not show utilization at all. ## 1. One UI 7.0 ## 2. One UI 8.x ### Steps to reproduce the behavior. Test environment: - Termux bionic build of Shotcut - 4K@30fps video rendering workload - Termux 0.119.0-beta.3/ Termux-X11 environment - Additional verification with Geekbench inside a proot container Observed behavior by device during Shotcut video rendering: **Galaxy S21 Ultra (Exynos 2100)** - One UI 7.0: CPU cores 0 through 7 show utilization during rendering. All 8 CPU cores are used correctly. **Galaxy S24+ (Exynos 2400)** - One UI 7.0: CPU cores 0 through 9 show utilization during rendering. All 10 CPU cores are used correctly. - One UI 8.0 / 8.5(Beta): CPU cores 0 through 8 show utilization during rendering. Only 9 CPU cores are used. The big-core cluster shows no utilization. **Galaxy S24 Ultra (Snapdragon 8 Gen 3)** - One UI 7.0: CPU cores 0 through 7 show utilization during rendering. All 8 CPU cores are used correctly. **Galaxy S25 Ultra (Snapdragon 8 Elite)** - One UI 8.5(Beta): CPU cores 0 through 5 show utilization during rendering. Only 6 CPU cores are used. The big-core cluster shows no utilization. **Galaxy S26 Ultra (Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen5)** - One UI 8.5: CPU cores 0 through 5 show utilization during rendering. Only 6 CPU cores are used. The big-core cluster shows no utilization. **Galaxy Tab S9 Ultra (Snapdragon 8 Gen2)** - One UI 8.0: CPU cores 0 through 6 show utilization during rendering. Only 7 CPU cores are used. The big-core cluster shows no utilization. ### What is the expected behavior? Additional observation: On One UI 8.0 / 8.5, I could not observe any utilization of the big-core cluster in any tested Termux or proot workload, not just during video rendering. Additional verification: - Geekbench inside a proot container also appears to be affected on One UI 8.5. - I also tested Good Lock Game Booster+ by adding Termux as a game app and setting CPU/GPU to maximum performance, but it did not change this behavior. Why this seems important: This does not look like a Shotcut-only issue. It appears to affect Termux native/proot workloads more broadly on One UI 8.x. Possible cause: This may be related to changed task classification, scheduler behavior, cpuset/cgroup assignment, or power policy on One UI 8.x. Please let me know if there is any debugging information I can provide. Thank you. ### System information * Termux application version: Termux 0.119.0-beta.3 * Android OS version: One UI 8.x(Android 16) / One UI 7.0(Android 15) * Device model: Galaxy S21 Ultra / Galaxy S24+ / Galaxy S24 Ultra / Galaxy S25 Ultra / Galaxy S26 Ultra / Galaxy Tab S9 Ultra
title: [Bug]: One UI 8.x: big CPU cores are never used in tested Termux/proot workloads

author: Zweih
body:
### Problem description

When returning to Termux from launchers, the soft keyboard is not restored to the state it was in before leaving. If the keyboard was visible, it briefly appears then immediately disappears. Tapping the terminal or the Keyboard button in the drawer restores it. Less consistently, if the keyboard was hidden it may reappear on return.

I did some digging to figure out why the recents switcher behaves differently and traced it to `setSoftKeyboardState ` in `TermuxTerminalViewClient.java`. Theres a fixed 300 ms delay before calling `showSoftInput`, assuming window focus has arrived by then. When Android delivers focus late, which happens whenever Termux is brought forward via `startActivity` (rather than the recents switchers `moveTaskToFront`), the IMM either ignores the call or a subsequent focus event hides the keyboard again. The recents switcher does not trigger this because it delivers focus before `onResume`, but tapping the app icon in the launcher does not.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

1. Open Termux and let the soft keyboard appear (type anything)
2. Press the Home button
3. Return to Termux by tapping its icon in the app launcher
4. The keyboard flickers briefly and disappears, or does not appear at all

NOTE: Returning via the recents switcher does not reproduce the issue

### What is the expected behavior?

The soft keyboard should be in the same state as when Termux was left. Visible if it was visible, hidden if it was hidden. It should be consistent with the behaviour when returning via the recents switcher.

### System information

* Termux application version: googleplay.2026.02.11
* Android OS version: 16 (API 36)
* Device model: Pixel 10
title: [Bug]: Soft keyboard flickers and disappears when returning to Termux

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

Device is Android 15. Install Termux and Syncthing, version prior to 2.0.15 work as expected.
Is it possible to force an earlier version?

### What is the expected behavior?

_No response_

### System information

* Termux application version:
* Android OS version:
* Device model:
title: [Bug]: Syncthing fails after upgrade to version 2.0.15

author: mikegilchrist
body:
## Feature Request

**Describe the desired behavior**
Add an orientation setting to the Termux Settings UI (accessible via the
left-side drawer) that allows users to lock the app to portrait or landscape
mode, independent of the system auto-rotate setting.

**Suggested options**
- Auto (follow system setting) -- default, current behavior
- Portrait (locked)
- Landscape (locked)

**Why this is useful**
The system auto-rotate toggle affects all apps globally. Users who want
auto-rotate enabled for other apps but prefer Termux to stay in portrait
(or landscape) have no per-app option today. Termux is commonly used while
doing other things and unintentional rotation disrupts the terminal session.

**Implementation note**
This could also be exposed as a `terminal-orientation` property in
`termux.properties` for consistency with the existing configuration
file approach, in addition to the Settings UI toggle.

**Environment**
- Termux version: (from F-Droid or GitHub releases)
- Android version: 14
- Device: Pixel 6a
title: Feature request: per-app orientation lock in Settings UI

body:
### Feature description

Currently there's no way to send any numpad input through termux, I would love to have the option to distinguish numpad numbers from other keyboard number keys and more importantly whether an arrow was typed into the keyboard or numpad keys.


Currently the supported extra keys configurable are: ctrl, alt, fn, shift, space, esc, tab, home

author: No-One-Rudro
body:
### Feature description

**Summary:**  
Termux should provide **runtime, optional toggles** for granting camera and microphone access, enabling scripts and Debian/proot apps accessed via Termux (e.g., via RVNC/RVC Viewer) to safely use device hardware while keeping permissions opt-in.

---

### ≡ƒöæ Key Features

- **Camera Access Toggle** ΓÇô Enable or disable camera use for scripts and apps.
- **Microphone Access Toggle** ΓÇô Enable or disable microphone use for scripts and apps.
- **Runtime Permission Requests** ΓÇô When a script calls an API needing hardware, Termux prompts the user with a clear dialog:
  - Script/App name requesting access  
  - Hardware requested (camera or mic)  
  - Buttons: Γ£à Allow / Γ¥î Deny
- **Status Indicators** ΓÇô Quick icon in Termux showing permission state:
  - ≡ƒöÆ Locked ΓåÆ Permissions disabled  
  - Γ£à Enabled ΓåÆ Hardware available  
  - Optional tooltip showing recent script access

---

### ≡ƒÆí Benefits

- Allows **advanced automation, AI scripts, and multimedia workflows**.  
- Keeps **security intact**: permissions are optional and transparent.  
- Compatible with **Debian/proot apps and RVNC/RVC Viewer setups**.  
- Follows **modern Android runtime permission practices**.  

---

### ≡ƒÄ» Use Cases

1. Scripts that call `termux-camera-photo` or `termux-microphone-record`.  
2. Debian GUI apps running via Termux using camera/mic input.  
3. AI or IoT projects needing voice or video input without compromising security.



### Additional information

### ≡ƒöù Does another app/terminal emulator have this feature?

- Currently, no known terminal emulator on Android provides **runtime, optional toggles for camera and microphone access** in the same way.  
- Android apps in general use **runtime permission dialogs**, which can serve as a model.  
- This feature is intended to bring **modern Android permission handling to Termux**, enabling scripts and Debian/proot apps to safely access hardware.

### ≡ƒîÉ References / Background

- Android Runtime Permissions: https://developer.android.com/training/permissions/requesting  
- Termux API Tools: https://wiki.termux.com/wiki/Termux:API
title: [Feature]: Optional Camera and Microphone Permission Toggles

author: JacobTDC
body:
### Feature description

Termux should declare the `android.permission.MANAGE_VIRTUAL_MACHINE` permission in it's manifest. While it is a `signtaure|privileged` permission, the AVF allows granting it via. ADB, and it would allow usage of the `/apex/com.android.virt/bin/vm` utility and related system services to create, manage, and interact with VMs within Termux.

### Additional information

More details about the Android Virtualization Framework can be found at the following sources:
https://android.googlesource.com/platform/packages/modules/Virtualization/+/refs/heads/main/libs/framework-virtualization/README.md
https://source.android.com/docs/core/virtualization
title: [Feature]: declare MANAGE_VIRTUAL_MACHINE permission

### Problem description

When trying to install nix with the multi-user installation inside a debian proot-distro instance, during the installation process, the whole Termux application suddenly crashes with no errors. I have been able to get this to happen consistently.

* Android OS version: 16 (OneUI 8.0)
* Device model: Samsung Galaxy A54

author: davidhedlund
body:
### Feature description

### Description  
The default Termux package repository (`termux-main`) currently includes some nonfree packages. For example, `proot-distro` maintains popular but nonfree GNU/Linux distributions. This mixing of free and nonfree software goes against the principle of repository separation and can create licensing and redistribution concerns.  

### Steps to Reproduce  
1. Review the default repository configuration at `$PREFIX/etc/apt/sources.list`, which contains:  
   `deb https://mirrors.cbrx.io/apt/termux/termux-main stable main`  
2. Comment out the above line and run:  
   `apt update && apt show proot-distro`  
   The package `proot-distro` will no longer appear, confirming it resides in `termux-main`.  

### Expected Behavior  
Nonfree packages should reside in a separate repository (e.g., `termux-nonfree`) to clearly distinguish between free and nonfree software.  

### Actual Behavior  
`termux-main` currently includes both free and nonfree packages, such as `proot-distro`.  

### Proposed Solution  
- Move nonfree packages (e.g., `proot-distro`) out of `termux-main` into a dedicated `termux-nonfree` repository.  
- Update documentation and `sources.list` templates accordingly.  

### References  
1. Verification: When the `termux-main` source line is commented out, `apt show proot-distro` fails, confirming its presence in that repository.

### Additional information

@robertkirkman Please take a look at this.
title: [Feature]: Separate nonfree packages into a distinct Termux repository

author: mrkrsl
body:
### Feature description

## Issue and use-case

The Text Input View (the swipe typing-compatible text input that can be switched into the extended keyboard area) is a great workaround for the limitations of text entry into the Terminal grid. It allows for the entry of long text using standard Android keyboard interactions into README files or for longer Markdown editing and writing.

The current Text Input View is basic. It allows only for a single, non-breaking line of text which removes context from what has been typed. It takes on a generic style where the text is 3x larger than the terminal text.

It could be easily improved so that it is more comfortable to type into to edit long-text.

## Feature request

Either have more sensible defaults for, or allow the user to override the default settings for the Text Input View:

1. Allowing multi-line text input that wraps and scrolls (e.g. `android:inputType="textMultiLine"` in place of `android:inputType="text"` and `android:maxLines` to be >1 - e.g. `3` or `5`).
2. The ability to control the font size so that more text can be displayed in the text area. The default could be the same size as that in the Terminal view itself.

See [the target resource here](https://github.com/termux/termux-app/blob/53f75a8da3b823c18a8244b298da50e87382984d/app/src/main/res/layout/view_terminal_toolbar_text_input.xml#L4).

Ideally the defaults could be overridden in the `~/.termux/termux.preferences` file and be minimally documented. 

This would greatly improve the Text Input View feature.

### Additional information

I believe Termux is unique in offering the Text Input View for long text input. It is a differentiator.

See [this Reddit thread](https://www.reddit.com/r/termux/comments/1q35hwz/how_to_config_text_input_view/) for context.
title: [Feature]: Configure Text Input View

author: henrebotha
body:
### Feature description

I would like to automate various aspects of my sessions. To that end, I need access to some CLI commands that can perform these actions:

- Create session
- Rename a given session
- End a given session
- Switch to a given session

### Additional information

The closest analogue I'm aware of is something like iTerm's Tmux integration, which essentially allows you to control GUI windows/tabs/splits using the Tmux CLI.
title: [Feature]: CLI commands to manage sessions

author: Palloxin
body:
### Problem description

So I use termux to mass replace text in files with regex.

The problem is, every time I send the command to replace, the sd card loses free space. And that space is undetectable by normal file explorers, even windows explorer.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

Install termux, give storage permission, and then ``cd`` a path with html files, or txt idk.

Then u use this command to replace the text:
``find . -type f -name "*.html" -exec sed -i -E 's/\baabb//g' {} +``

Note: I have the sd card set as mi main storage location.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5ecc0344-db28-4910-8052-7784dd814456

In the video u can noticed th free space going from 45.20Gb to 45.08Gb after the replacement.

I am reporting because I suddenly found my sd card free space to absolutely 0 bytes, I did everything from clearing cache and disintalling termux and other apps, nothing the ghist space couldnt be found.

So I put the sd into a pc and analized it with a specific program and...

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b6d75320-0827-440b-8905-da11c782a1ad)

I was finally able to get the space back.


note that the amount of files it checked dont even amount to 30mb, while the soace increases by 120mb every time
### What is the expected behavior?

Every replacement doesnt occupy more space, or at least it is cleaned right afterward automatically.

or

-if the app is unistalled, that occupied space must be freed, which didnt happen

### System information

* Termux application version: 0.118.2
* Android OS version: android 8 miui 8
* Device model: huaw p8 lite 2017
title: [Bug]: Cumulative ghost space in sd card

author: twaik
body:
### Feature description

I would like to propose a feature request for Termux related to passwordless SSH authentication based on explicit local user approval.

Tailscale provides a convenient SSH mechanism where authentication is handled via the Tailscale account itself rather than system passwords, PAM, OAuth, or external identity providers. This enables passwordless SSH with clear access control and user-side confirmation.

I propose considering a similar approach for Termux. The idea is that when an incoming SSH connection attempt occurs, Termux could send a local notification to the user. Opening the notification would present a dialog explaining that a remote client is attempting to establish an SSH session, with options to allow or deny the connection. If allowed, the connection would be established without using passwords, keys, or traditional authentication mechanisms, or a one-time session authorization could be issued.

This mechanism would allow SSH usage in a passwordless mode based on physical access to the device and explicit user consent. It could serve as an alternative to passwords and keys, and be useful in scenarios where quick, controlled access is needed without prior SSH key or credential setup.

### Additional information

N/A.
title: [Feature]: termux passwordless ssh server

author: ssvb
body:
### Feature description

The look of many text mode user interfaces of Linux applictions had been specifically designed for the [de-facto standard text mode VGA palette](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VGA_text_mode), which every IBM PC compatible computer uses by default after booting. One of the Linux applications sensitive to the color scheme is [Midnight Commander](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Midnight_Commander) (the classic old appearance). In particular, syntax highlighting in the built-in Midnight Commander's editor is barely readable when using any other color scheme.

These are the desired color values:
```
# https://github.com/ThomasDickey/ncurses-snapshots/blob/master/test/linux-color.dat

color0: #000000
color1: #AA0000
color2: #00AA00
color3: #AA5500
color4: #0000AA
color5: #AA00AA
color6: #00AAAA
color7: #AAAAAA
color8: #555555
color9: #FF5555
color10: #55FF55
color11: #FFFF55
color12: #5555FF
color13: #FF55FF
color14: #55FFFF
color15: #FFFFFF
```

### Additional information

MATE Terminal offers this color scheme under the name 'linuxconsole'.
title: [Feature]: Use the de-facto standard VGA color palette as the default color scheme for termux

author: Merith-TK
body:
### Feature description

A wearos app that is a trimmed down version of termux.

Basically I just want to be able to have termux on my smart watch and the current methods kinda suck in terms of actually doing it.

There is the ADB side loading, which sucks because it forces you to use ADB over tcp which is a pain when the watch keeps going to sleep. Then you gotta use a tool like scrcpy or ADB keyboard inputs to install an ssh server, and that only works on WiFi if the watch is even deciding to connect to WiFi (wearos can be finicky depending on device)

But I did notice apps, even third party android apks, can side load an app onto the watch directly through the phone linked to the watch.

So in my head I'm thinking it could be possible to add an companion app (like termux API) for wearos, and then add a termux-wearos package to termux, and have that package be how you interact, such as a special ssh like link or something, or just sending scripts to run like termux-widget does

### Additional information

No real additional info yet, will get back to you with references after work today
title: [Feature]: Wear OS Companion

author: IntinteDAO
body:
### Feature description

This is a concept that isn't talked about much, but perhaps it should be.

In short, if you don't have root, tutorials suggest doing it manually using adb.

In short, it often works like this: you enable ADB network debugging, connect to your device using adb, and issue commands.

LADB is no different here, as it does the same thing, but automatically. It enables network debugging and <potentially> executes commands (makes commands available to you). This solution isn't without its drawbacks, as you obviously need to be connected to Wi-Fi (as with ADB network debugging), but it would allow (if implemented in Termux) reconfiguration of Android and disabling unnecessary security features without user intervention (just pairing with ADB is enough).

Of course, restarting the phone doesn't affect LADB's operation. This means that if a similar functionality to Termux were implemented, even system updates might not break it. But if something were to happen, the user would be informed.

### Additional information

[LADB](https://github.com/tytydraco/ladb)
title: [Feature]: Bypassing Phantom Killer Using a LADB-Like Solution

author: groszdaniel
body:
### Problem description

If Termux is launched in certain ways ΓÇö from a shortcut from termux-widget, or via a termux-url-opener hook ΓÇö, it does not respect the background color specified in ~/.termux/colors.properties, and starts with a black background. Weirdly, it does respect the foreground color, so if I use a black-on-white style, the script's output is invisible.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

Choose a color scheme that has a background color other than black, particularly a light scheme.

Put a script in ~/.shortcuts. Put the Termux widget on the home screen. Launch the script from the widget.

The bug is only reproduced if there's no Termux session already running. It also doesn't manifest when launching Termux via its normal app icon.
The bug happens most of the time under these conditions, but not always.
Also, while the terminal initially has a black background when this happens, if I switch to another app and back, or even just open the app switcher (Recents screen) and go back to Termux, it then gets the correct background.

### What is the expected behavior?

The background is as specified in the color scheme, e.g. light if it's a light scheme.

### System information

* Termux application version: 0.118.3 (1002)
* Android OS version: crDroid 10.13, based on Android 14
* Device model: Xiaomi Redmi 9C NFC
title: [Bug]: Background color not respected when started with scripts

author: GfEW
body:
### Feature description #### The Problem First off, it's awesome pinch-zooming works so well in `termux`. Adjusting the font size also allows to quickly adjust the terminal size to specific needs. Unfortunately, as of v0.118.3, you can't easily recognize the actual size you're adjusting the terminal to. #### Suggested Solution During pinch-zooming and then sticking for two seconds or so, display a small geometry indicator on top of the current terminal. ### Additional information - Some `xterm`s display the actual terminal size (usually rows x cols because it's a terminal; there may be a few that show cols x rows instead) when the window size is changed. - The best workaround in `termux` v.0.118.3 that I know is frequently running `stty size` while resizing, or even `watch stty size`. However, doing so requires you - to empty the command line (unless you haven't hit the sessions limit yet and can start a new session for that purpose), - then to type in that `stty` command every time (or setting up a readline binding to do so). - Otherwise, starting the actual program that requires a certain line or column count would frequently just tell "nope, that's too few", or you'll be using smaller font than necessary because the terminal has ended up larger than required. Note that not every CLI/TUI program handles SIGWINCH properly, so you'd have to quit the program, pinch-zoom, then test again. All in all, that procedure would be even more tedious than the workaround above.
EDIT 20251102: Cleaned up duplications due to FR template.
title: [Feature]: Indicate `stty size` While Resizing

author: Cletip
body:
### Problem description

Hello,

I just want to get access, as specified in the wiki, to the ΓÇ£Termux-private folderΓÇ¥ inside a external drive (usb in my case) :

[Internal and external storage](https://wiki.termux.com/wiki/Internal_and_external_storage)

Symlink to a Termux-private folder on external storage (only if external storage is available).
~/storage/external-1

My setup:
Termux 0.118.3
Android 16, GrapheneOS
USB drive formatted in exFAT, correctly recognized by the standard file explorer
IΓÇÖve granted Termux all media access permissions.
I ran the command ΓÇ£termux-setup-storageΓÇ¥, and the shared, etc. folders were created.

IΓÇÖve already checked some related issues, such as:

#71 
#3120
#3647 

But nothing is completely clear.

The clearest explanation is from @agnostic-apollo, who wrote:

> The */Android/data/com.termux paths on stable storages are created when getExternalFilesDirs() is called. It wonΓÇÖt be created for external hard drives like already mentioned. You can use the mount command to search for mounted volumes. It should ideally be mounted under /mnt/media_rw as is and then likely mounted with sdcardfs under /storage for apps to use with SAF.

However, I know that on my Android 13 device with Termux 0.118.1, this folder external-1 exists, and I can access my USB drive with external-1. So why isnΓÇÖt that the case here?
Is it due to Android 16? Or GrapheneOS? Or other factors ?

And, I have access to /mnt/media_rw/XXXX-XXXX/, but I can't do a simple 
`git clone  /mnt/media_rw/XXXX-XXXX/path/to/a/gitdirectory $HOME/locale/`
Why ?

Another related but secondary question: the wiki says,
ΓÇ£External storage: external SD cards or USB hard drives. Typically read-only, except the Termux private directory.ΓÇ¥
Does that mean the folder should be accessible just like the internal one?
If this is indeed an issue, would it at least be possible to clarify the wiki?

Thanks in advance for your help.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

* USB drive formatted in exFAT, correctly recognized by the standard file explorer
* Grant Termux all media access permissions.
* Run ΓÇ£termux-setup-storageΓÇ¥
* Say "yes", and check that there is no "storage/external-1"

### What is the expected behavior?

"external-1" folder being created, to access the 

### System information

* Termux application version: 0.118.3
* Android OS version: 16 (Grapheneos)
* Device model: Pixel 9 Pro XL
title: [Bug]: termux-setup-storage doesnΓÇÖt create external-1 for USB drive on GrapheneOS

author: Bariyasachin96
body:
### Feature description

I am a blind user who depends on a screen reader (TalkBack) to use Termux. The app currently has serious accessibility issues that make it very difficult for visually impaired users to work effectively.

Issues

1. Cursor movement not spoken

When moving the cursor inside the terminal, the screen reader does not announce the text at the new position.

The cursor moves, but no speech feedback is given.



2. Output text not accessible

When new output appears in the terminal, it is visible, but moving the cursor over it does not cause the screen reader to read anything.

This makes it impossible to review terminal output with a screen reader.



3. Missing session management options

The menu does not provide options to create a new session, switch between sessions, or close sessions.

There is no accessible way to manage multiple sessions.





Suggestions

1. Fix cursor handling so that whenever the cursor moves, the screen reader reads the text at that position.


2. Ensure that all terminal output can be reviewed by moving the cursor, with proper speech feedback.


3. Add accessible menu options for session management (new session, switch session, close session).




---

Impact:
These issues make Termux nearly unusable for blind users. Fixing cursor reading, output accessibility, and session management will greatly improve usability and inclusivity for visually impaired users.




### Additional information

/
title: [Feature]: Accessibility Improvements

author: theamericanaccount
body:
### Problem description

Hello,

I've got this issue at a certain point one year ago when I started publishing [DogeOS](https://github.com/themartiancompany/dogeos) cross-platform videogames packages, i.e. packages which seamlessly build on Termux, Arch Linux and Windows using a single build script and which are published on the [Ur](https://github.com/themartiancompany/ur), the uncensorable undeletable application store, which is hopefully going to convince all maintainers of the aforementioned environments to stop working for free while still releasing sources with a copyleft license, such that I noticed (obviously) other Android applications can't access Termux storage unless than through Android mechanisms.

As a workaround for this issue I've written a program called `termux-install-shared`, included in the latest version of `reallymakepkg`, the program which builds cross-platform Ur packages in place of `makepkg`.

In practice it installs data in an application-specific Android shared directory while linking to it at the correct termux data location.
This way one can for example share icon files between freedesktop-compatible launchers and android-compotible termux shortcut launchers.

So nothing, I wanted to report it exists as it solves quite a tricky issue one had to deal with manually and per-package up to now.
Normally I would have directly sent a package integration merge request, but at the same time I'm also selling the application on the store, which by the way its currently the only application store for Termux.

I'm also working on integrating standard linux applications into android (yeah before google), as in each application gets its own window and its undistinguishable from Android apps, which I'm very near to complete.

In that regard I'd like to likely discuss if there's a way to have that into Termux for users to enjoy, as it's very probably a much requested feature, but keeping on this silly requirement I have that people pay me for my work.

Some contributors have suggested to have the store included in base packages, still I'm not quite sure how that can work well until Termux will ship with apt, as debian packages doesn't seem to me aren't built cross-platform very easily on-device.

The idea of having to make a paid better Termux and call it DogeOS Android base instead of just 'merging' the two and have its contributors get paid for their work I frankly don't like much.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

Use an intent to open a termux file results in access denied.

### What is the expected behavior?

The same one gets when using `termux-open` (from which you should remove the `xdg-open` provides/symlink as it conflicts with FreeDesktop one).

### System information

* Termux application version: latest
* Android OS version: DogeOS
* Device model: Human Instrumentality Project 00, 01 and 02
title: [Fix]: Share data between Termux and Android applications

author: ptoon1911

### Feature description

A combined window in Termux that provides:
- Auto-generate PIN functionality
- History log of generated PINs with option to export as Excel/PDF
- Copy generated PIN to clipboard
- Send PIN to Android devices via ADB (with multi-device support)
This should all be available within a single interface for efficient device management.

### Additional information

Some device management or security apps offer PIN generation and export, but an integrated workflow with ADB multi-device support is uncommon. See tools like KeePass for PIN generation, but not with ADB/mobile integration. Export features are common in password managers.

author: ScreenNamePlus1
body:
### Problem description

Okay so I have permox installed on my Motorola and i have them running Android 15. I'm not sure some people have said this maybe because of Android 14 and newer versions but the turnbox app was just updated recently so I don't think that's the problem. On my Motorola device is termucks does not display colors from oh my zsh. I'm running power level 10K at the theme and it should have colors. It doesn't do that unless I highlight it but it's only on my Motorola phone. My pixel 3A XL has four color but that has Android 12 or maybe 13. My Motorola phone running Android 13 has this problem and my Motorola that has ran both 14 and 15 also has this problem. I only get the colors that I specify in my dsh file if I highlight the text in the termux terminal. 

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

Install all my dsh on a Motorola and try to change the theme to power level 10K and it won't do anything except black and white unless you highlight the text. It's been like that on every Motorola I've ever had. 

### What is the expected behavior?

Well it's supposed to display different colors for the theme that I installed them with all my ZSH you know different color text. I even tried manually editing the zsh file but that didn't work either.

### System information

* Termux application version:
* Android OS version: 15
* Device model: Motorola Moto G 2024

I found the problem... settings/display/display size and text/ tirn high contrast off... duh. That was a lpt of trouble for one little issue.
title: [Bug]: zsh colors

author: groszdaniel
body: ### Feature description I suggest adding a feature to search in the output of programs (really just the contents of the terminal). One can always pipe to `grep` or `less`, but in some cases (such as if you've already executed the program, or it's a program continuously running in the foreground, or you're unsure if it prints the relevant output to stdout or stderr) it's more convenient if the terminal program also provides a search feature. ### Additional information Several terminal programs, such as Konsole and GNOME Terminal* have it, attaching screenshot of Konsole as an example. * Not the same as its GNOME HIG-compliant replacement GNOME Console, which doesn't have it.
title: [Feature]: Search in terminal contents (program output)

author: Digicrat
body:
### Problem description

I upgraded to the Samsung Flip7, and have just started playing around with Termux in it with the new Dex Mode (One UI8).

I'm not sure how much of this is a Termux vs a Dex issue (I suspect a bit of both), but several times now I've had Termux crash when switching between Dex and non-Dex, or simply re-opening Dex mode later.

The typical case I'm seeing is, once Dex-mode is reopened:
- Termux font sizes are oddly super-zoomed. (Note: I also see this periodically on Chrome, and in that app re-launching within Dex is my only option to restore normal zoom levels)
- Termux is non-responsive to any keyboard inputs or attempts to adjust zoom
- After a short period of attempting to get it to respond, Termux just exits/crashes.
- I'm not sure what, if any, log files may be useful, but I'm happy to collect some.


### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

- Open Termux natively
- Connect an external display and access Termux.  Sometimes this will start with the issue already present on the first attempt.
- Switch back to native mode
- Re-launch Dex mode.  If it hasn't previously, termux text will be zoomed-in to max levels, interface will be non-responsive, and it will likely exit/crash within a few seconds.

### What is the expected behavior?

It should automatically scale appropriately to the display when switching to/from Dex mode and not crash.

### System information

* Termux application version: 0.118.3 (from app info)
* Android OS version: 16
* Device model: Samsung Flip7

### Possibly Related? Edit: Not related
I've seen the crash described above with only termux running, however I also just had a different crash when trying to run xfce with termux-x11.  I don't think these are related, but in that case I see a few warnings about "Failed to get system bus: Could not connect NO such file or desktop" and "xlib: extension DPMS missing on display" before Termux reports `[Process completed (signal 9) - press Enter]`

Edit: [Found the docs on this issue](https://github.com/agnostic-apollo/Android-Docs/blob/master/en/docs/apps/processes/phantom-cached-and-empty-processes.md#how-to-disable-the-phantom-processes-killing), and toggling the option seems to have resolved this part.  I haven't tried toggling Dex mode again to see if it had any affect on the primary issue.
title: [Bug]: Crash when switching between Dex and non-Dex

You'll notice it begins with "illegal instruction".

They all do.

Why would an instruction *become* illegal, suddenly?

possible security-policy-change, in Android?

I don't know.

I DO know that this now has broken my learning-environment ( both math & programming ), so now I can't have the means needed within Termux.

What else it's broken by this sudden change, I don't know..

I've reinstalled Julia repeatedly, I've reinstalled distro-images repeatedly ( I now have an archive-out/wipe/install/repopulate script for Ubuntu, & am building the needed one for Arch, because all this testing has required sooo damn many permutations ), but that "illegal instruction" problem is breaking something, and I've no idea where the cause is.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

install either Ubuntu or Arch, within Termux, using proot-distro install

as root, get them set-up enough to be usable, with regular user-accounts,

download the official Julia binaries, either the 1.10 LTS version or the 1.11 current version,

create /usr/local/stow

unpack them in there, then xstow or stow the one you want to try ( this breakage happens with either )

switch to your regular-user account, within that distro,

```
julia

]  #  that switches into Pkg, the package-manager

add  DataFrames  #  OR
add  Pluto  #  OR
add  Revise  #  OR
add  OhMyREPL  #  etc..
# just in the last few days, MANY packages became impossible-to-install,
```
& since Julia itself didn't change,

.. then what did?

I'd already reported this problem to Pluto, because it was the 1st affected,

and I'd thought it was a problem with their package,

but within a day or so, it was damn-near-everything in my startup.jl file which couldn't work, anymore..

To see the errors that happened during the attempted-precompilation, you let the Pkg.add("Pluto"),
( that is the way you'd do it if you weren't in the REPL, )

.. with its special-package-manager-mode, crash ..

then you get out from the package-manager,

& you type

```
err
```

which gets you the kind of stuff like what I quoted, above.

### What is the expected behavior?

1 week ago, Julia WORKED in Termux's Ubuntu!

NO "illegal instruction" problem, at all!

Now it's broken in both distros I tried, the same way, which *isn't likely*, you know?

### System information

* Termux application version: 118.3
* Android OS version: 15
* Device model: Samsung tablet, ARMv9 cores.

I owe Pluto.jl an apology: it wasn't their project which created the problem, & I'm sorry if I'm bothering you with this when it may have been created by Android, but *I've lost my homeschool/self-education*, and .. aarrrgggghhhh!!

Infrastructure should *just work*, you know?

Anyways, IF it's created by some change in Termux, THEN I hope this helps get it fixed.

IF it's created by a change in Android, .. then I have to get a silent computer to use, instead of Termux, because they've broken this as a usable-for-learning means.

Me & everybody in the world who was using this for our math+Julia study..

_ /\ _
title: [Bug]: Affects both Termux's Ubuntu & Arch, so it's a Termux bug: Julia's binary aarch64 now can't compile many packages.

author: Rudxain
body:
### Problem description

- mathiasbynens/dotfiles#930
- #4652

### Steps to reproduce the behavior.

```sh
PS1='\[\e[1m\]\w\n$ \[\e[0m\]'
```
The entire prompt will be bold, until we resize the terminal (pinch-to-zoom).

The weird thing is that, when it's [my dotfiles'](https://github.com/Rudxain/dotfiles/) prompt, the 2nd line is **never bold** (no need to resize). Here's a copy of the file, because the commit-hashes are unstable:
```sh
#!/bin/sh

case "$TERM" in
	xterm-color|*-256color) color_support=y;;
	*) color_support=n;;
esac

last_exit() {
	local e=$?
	[ $e -ne 0 ] && \
		printf "${1:-}%02x${2:-} " $e
	return $e
}

if [ $color_support = y ]; then
	PS1="\n\[\e[1m\]\$(last_exit \"\[\e[31m\]\" \"\[\e[0m\]\[\e[1m\]\")"
else
	PS1="\n\$(last_exit)"
fi
PS1="$PS1\w"

if command -v git >/dev/null; then
	git_b_name() {
		git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree >/dev/null 2>&1 || return
		echo " // $(\
			git symbolic-ref --quiet --short HEAD 2>/dev/null || \
			git describe --all --exact-match HEAD 2>/dev/null || \
			git rev-parse --short HEAD 2>/dev/null || \
			echo '(?)' \
		)"
	}
	PS1="$PS1\$(git_b_name)"
fi

PS1="$PS1\n╬ö "
[ $color_support = y ] && PS1="$PS1\[\e[0m\]"
unset color_support
```

### What is the expected behavior?

Both lines of the prompt should be bold at all times:
> **\~
> $** 

Instead, this is the actual prompt after resizing the window:
> **\~**
> $ 


### System information

* Termux application version: 0.119.0-beta.3
* Android OS version: 14 or 8
* Device model: Irrelevant?
title: [Bug]: escape-seq styling lost after resizing

author: Erhannis
body:
### Feature description

I found a series of commands (a modification of https://gist.github.com/Cyang39/917f59062fc9d57302d94e3fc534fb16 ) intended for Termux, that I can use to download soundtracks from my Steam account, a feature I notice is frequently requested of their app.  However, it's pretty involved, and would be nice to wrap in an app.  However, it involved several layers of chroot-ing and a (potentially) interactive shell program that takes like 30 seconds to start up (SteamCMD, x86 emulated via FEX-Emu).  My vision was to attach an app to a virtual shell, and the app would provide buttons with standard actions (install dependencies, start the session, log in, list albums, etc) that would inject commands into stdin and perform limited parsing of stdout, as well as mirroring output for the user to see in case something goes wrong or it e.g. wants them to log in again or w/e.  However, I don't see a way to do that - RUN_COMMAND runs a single command, waits for it to complete, and returns the result.  I _might_ be able to write the app to work with standalone scripts, but I think it'll be slower (having to boot the environment on every action) and less fault-recoverable (since the user can't see errors until after the script has shut down).  Is there an existing way to get access to an interactive session from a third-party app?  Some workaround perhaps, like starting a script that redirects to some sockets, or somethings?  I'm not sure what Android's security model permits.  (Or, is there a way to initialize a Termux environment in a manually created Process?)  If not, how difficult would this be to implement?  Say, you send stdin intents with specified session IDs, and every N milliseconds any new stdout/stderr gets sent back.

### Additional information

Approximate goal: https://gist.github.com/Cyang39/917f59062fc9d57302d94e3fc534fb16 , and being able to interactively run Termux > FEXDroid (on Ubuntu) > FEXBash > SteamCMD.
title: [Feature]: interactive RUN_COMMAND

author: shankarkrupa
body:
### Feature description

I want to be able to invoke a script in Termux when I share it from other apps. Specific scripts of relevance are termux-file-editor and termux-url-opener scripts.

Invoking this script currently requires the **display over other apps** permission. However, this is not possible in all the Android versions (eg: Android Go 13) in specific mobile phone models.

It will therefore be quite useful if a workaround is provided to still accept the received parameters and run in foreground/background without needing this permission.

### Additional information

Not sure if other emulators provide this option. There are workarounds suggested to enable the Display over other apps permission through adb but even that does not work in the specific phone model I came across (POCO C51).

A few other apps are able to be invoked with the passed information from Android Sharesheet like WhatsApp, browsers, etc. in the same phone model. I therefore hope this could be  sorted out within Termux App circumventing the need for this permission.
title: [Feature]: Support to run scripts when invoked through sharing to Termux from other apps (Android Go)

author: locuturus
body:
### Feature description

I'd like a way to share a URI to Termux as just the text of the intent. It could be json or something like that. 

I imagine being in some file manager of choice, observing a file I should run through ffmpeg or exiftool or imagemagick or what have you, and I send the text data of the intent to a script I've already set up. The part I'm interested in is usually file:///something or content:blahblah. I'm happy to do the substitutions in my script to convert different URIs into real paths but I haven't found a way to actually get the intent strings into a script from the share menu. I really don't need or want to copy the file for this workflow.

### Additional information

#2658 is somewhat related in spirit.

Assuming this gets traction I'd next want additional share targets whether by a modal dialogue box or the more limited way apps can list 3 or 4 share targets right in the share sheet. I guess they could be set up a lot like how termux-file-editor works now. What I'm starting to do now is copy the filepath in supported file managers and run scripts from the widget that first grab the clipboard. It's clunky going to the home screen every time but it works. Being able to share a filepath, even a content formatted URI that needs to be rebuilt, to those scripts and never leave the first app would be aces.
title: [Feature]: Pass URI Intent as Text to Termux
