Although I could also use ADB it was always to cumbersome to choose device by IP (I sometimes have up to 4 devices hooked on my PC).
title: [Feature]: Zoom on device without touchscreen (WSA)

author: mglh
body:
### Feature description

I assume that swiping up and down sends \<cursor up\> and \<cursor down\> key presses to the program running in the terminal. This works well for programs like vim and newsboat, where swiping scrolls up and down the main screen content line-wise.

However, in other programs \<cursor up\> and \<cursor down\> key presses do browse through the command history, and scrolling is done page-wise with the \<page up\> and \<page down\> keys. Irssi is an example.

Further programs in turn may use other keys or key combinations for scrolling the screen content.

I would suggest to create a new termux-* tool which offers to change / customize the key presses sent by swiping, separately for each session. This way a shell alias could be set up to suit for a certain program.

Since page-wise scrolling needs much less key presses than line-wise scrolling, to be usable this method would require the number of key presses sent by swiping to be adjustable, too.

### Additional information

I admit that using terminal multiplexers like screen or tmux would lead to problems here, as they would allow multiple programs to be run within one session, each of which could have different requirements on the keys presses sent by swiping.
title: [Feature]: Scrolling by swiping in programs which expect <page up> and <page down> key presses for that

author: sk-Prime
body:
### Feature description

Currently, You can share single file to termux and then that file will be handled by `termux-file-editor` as it mentioned in here https://wiki.termux.com/wiki/Intents_and_Hooks 
#### Proposal
- Add capabilities to share multiple files.
- Add a mechanism to pass command line argument to that `termux-file-editor` so it will pass that argument to linked binary or script.
- Optional: `termux-open --file-chooser` command. it will return selected file as a string list. So inside python script, we can call like this (I don't know if it is possible or not)
```
import subprocess 
selected_files=subprocess.check_output(['termux-open --file-chooser']))
```
#### Why I want it
There are numerous application of this feature.
- Image to PDF:  Select multiple files from gallery and then share it to termux. `termux-file-editor` will call linked script, in this case a python script which can convert all those images to pdf.
- Image/documents batch processing
- File sharing service using python + flask

As termux-file-editor linked to a single script, Passing command line argument is crucial because it can direct behavior of that script. Those argument can tell what to do with those files. There can be a text entry box above edit button. Look at the image.

author: Fs00
body:
#### Feature description

Termux provides a great terminal UX, however it's a very big app (~150MB) for those users that don't take advantage of all of its features.
Actually, I use Termux only to run system commands as root (`pm`, `appops`, `am`, ecc.) from time to time and I'm not interested in installing packages or running Linux binaries. However, I **love** the extra keys row and just this feature is a strong enough motivation to use Termux simply as a terminal emulator.
Therefore, I think that a "lite"/"core" flavor of the app without package management nor pre-installed packages would better fit those people who want to use Termux just for its UX, without taking too much storage space.

Also, this alternative flavor would (theoretically) not be subject to the W^X restriction (#2155) and could be updated through the Play Store. 

#### Reference implementation

The "failsafe" mode of Termux is a good example of how a trimmed version of Termux could look like.
title: Feature: Lightweight flavor of the app without package management

author: rsola
body:
<!--
IMPORTANT:

1. Support of Android 5.x - 6.x is finished.
2. Fill the template AFTER comments.
-->

Hi. I have been using Termux for a long time and I find it very useful, but a few annoying issues surface from time to time. Some of them are easy to reproduce in order to avoid as much as possible the circumstances which trigger them. Some other issues are harder to track down.

**Problem description**
<!--
A clear and concise description of what the problem is.
You may post screenshots in addition to description.
-->
Suppose you have the default extra keys bar with the four arrow keys, or you customized it via termux.properties. Arrows, DEL and BKSP have autorepeat behavior, so they are sent continuously as long as you hold them. When two of these keys are tapped at the same time, autorepeat gets stuck.

**Steps to reproduce**
<!--
Steps to reproduce the behavior. Please post all necessary
commands that are needed to reproduce the issue.
-->
To make the problem more obvious, open a full screen editor with some text loaded. Then, tap (no need to hold or keep tapping) any two arrows or an arrow and DEL or BKSP at the same time on the extra keys bar. It may also work with DEL and BKSP if you have included them both on your extra keys settings.

For example, you are editing a file. A few consecutive lines have a comment character at the beginning and you want to remove it from all these lines (suppose your editor doesn't have a macro feature). Then, you repeatedly tap DEL and DOWN in rapid succession, which will trigger this issue. Cursor moves like crazy, history lines are recalled or characters are being deleted at great speed. There is just a little you can do to stop this madness. Ctrl and Alt extra keys cannot be used because they get cancelled whenever a key is sent to the terminal.

Workaround: long tap on screen so selection handles and the copy/paste/more menu appear. Then, autorepeat stops. However, a ghost handle or menu could remain on screen when dismissed.

**Expected behavior**
<!--
A clear and concise description of what you expected to happen.
-->
Autorepeat mode must not get stuck, i.e., maybe simultaneous key presses with autorepeat should be processed serially.

**Additional information**

* Termux application version: 0.114
* Android OS version: 8.1
* Device model: Samsung SM-J710F (Galaxy J7 from 2016)
title: Autorepeat gets stuck when pressing two arrows or arrow+DEL/BKSP at the same time on extra keys bar

author: iamahuman
body:
**Feature description**
Termux should circumvent Play Store policy of restricting execution of arbitrary code from third parties, by imitating what Google Chrome does. Bundling packages into APKs is certainly _not_ the way to go.

1. Consult Firefox and Chrome as to how it executes external code (e.g. JavaScript) while still managing to comply with Play Store policy.
2. Run user code in a sandbox (`isolated_app`?), emulating forbidden system calls as needed. (Note that we already do this with execve to handle `#!/usr/bin/...` shebangs).
   - Additional permissions to control system can be granted through some kind of broker, which user code can communicate to via IPC.
   - Don't rely exclusively on ptrace through; rather, approach the problem with a mix of ptrace+seccomp+recompilation.
3. Circumvent W^X restriction by using a custom binary loader (instead of e.g. `/system/bin/linker`).

**Reference implementation**

N/A

**Related**

- #655
- #1072
title: Revisit the Android W^X problem

author: ghost
body:
All Termux repositories previously hosted on JFrog Bintray are down since 1st May, 2021. This means that all repositories hosted on `dl.bintray.com` domain are not available anymore. There nothing can be done on our side because Bintray service [announced its sunset](https://jfrog.com/blog/into-the-sunset-bintray-jcenter-gocenter-and-chartcenter/).

```
Ign:2 https://dl.bintray.com/grimler/game-packages-24 games InRelease
Ign:3 https://dl.bintray.com/grimler/science-packages-24 science InRelease
Err:4 https://dl.bintray.com/grimler/game-packages-24 games Release
  403  Forbidden
Err:5 https://dl.bintray.com/grimler/science-packages-24 science Release
  403  Forbidden
Get:1 https://grimler.se/termux-packages-24 stable InRelease [13.1 kB]
Get:6 https://grimler.se/termux-packages-24 stable/main all Packages [21.6 kB]
Get:7 https://grimler.se/termux-packages-24 stable/main aarch64 Packages [269 kB]
Reading package lists... Done
E: The repository 'https://dl.bintray.com/grimler/game-packages-24 games Release' does not have a Release file.
N: Metadata integrity can't be verified, repository is disabled now.
N: Possible cause: repository is under maintenance or down (wrong sources.list URL?).
E: The repository 'https://dl.bintray.com/grimler/science-packages-24 science Release' does not have a Release file.
N: Metadata integrity can't be verified, repository is disabled now.
N: Possible cause: repository is under maintenance or down (wrong sources.list URL?).
```

## What you should do as Termux user (only for Android 7+)

You need to pick a one of the available mirrors by using utility `termux-change-repo` or manually edit `sources.list` by replacing the Bintray URL with value obtained from [Termux Mirrors List](https://github.com/termux/termux-packages/wiki/Mirrors).

**Beware of third-party scripts claiming theirselves as repository fixers! They could install untrusted GPG key and redirect your Termux installation onto malicious repository.** *Official tool for changing repositories is bundled within Termux and called `termux-change-repo`.*

Usage of `termux-change-repo` is simple:
1. Select one or more repositories for which you want to change mirror by tapping "space" and navigating over list by up/down arrow keys. Tap enter to confirm the choice.
  ![Screenshot_20210602-004033](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25881154/120394005-85c99700-c33b-11eb-998a-0797c0b74f7b.png)
2. Pick a mirror, it is recommended to begin with mirror hosted by Grimler. Same as previously, navigate over list by arrow keys, pick mirror by space key and confirm choice by pressing "enter".
  ![Screenshot_20210602-004103](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25881154/120394033-8feb9580-c33b-11eb-8c7a-cf440783abe4.png)


Highly recommended to run `pkg upgrade` after changing mirror.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25881154/116244521-ad43a780-a770-11eb-88c6-054fb1950bfd.mp4

### If you get `E: Repository .. changed its .. value from 'Bintray' to ..`

Package manager can show you a notification with prompt which look like this one:
```
E: Repository 'https://grimler.se/termux-root-packages-24 root InRelease' changed its 'Origin' value from 'Bintray' to 'termux-root-packages-24 root'
E: Repository 'https://grimler.se/termux-root-packages-24 root InRelease' changed its 'Label' value from 'Bintray' to 'termux-root-packages-24 root'
N: This must be accepted explicitly before updates for this repository can be applied. See apt-secure(8) manpage for details.
Do you want to accept these changes and continue updating from this repository? [y/N] 
```

It is completely normal. Answer "yes" to this prompt. It will be shown only once per repository, so package manager will ask you to confirm for `game-repo`, `science-repo`, etc separately but you will need to confirm them only one time.

***

## Do not use Termux app from Google Play. It is deprecated.

Use Termux application from F-Droid, here is the link: https://f-droid.org/en/packages/com.termux/. Google Play variant is deprecated, doesn't receive updates and contains issues as well as outdated repository URLs.

Before migrating to F-Droid Termux version, uninstall the application and all add-ons like Termux:API, Termux:Styling, Termux:Widget and others. This is a required step because of signature mismatch between F-Droid and Google Play variants. If you need to back up data before uninstalling app, you can use suggestions from https://wiki.termux.com/wiki/Backing_up_Termux.

***

## Remember that Android 5.x - 6.x is NOT SUPPORTED!

Android OS version range 5.x - 6.x has been deprecated since 01.01.2020 and no longer worked on anymore. Provided here repository workarounds are only for Android 7.0. We never announced that bringing back support of legacy Android versions.

If you managed to put an URL of our mirrors to your sources.list and upgrade your legacy Termux, do not complain about broken installation. *If maintainers marked something as unsupported, perhaps there is a reason for that, isn't it?*

Discussions about Android 5.x - 6.x are off-topic here.

## Remember that VMOS and F1VM environments are NOT SUPPORTED!

These "virtual" environments are not supported by Termux. They have a number of issues including broken `apt-key`.

Discussions about VMOS and F1VM are off-topic here.
title: [README] Bintray repository hosting shut down | Error 403 Forbidden | Error 404

author: aurium
body:
**Feature description**
A file header comment can describe how the file must be send to `~/bin/termux-file-editor`. Allowing us to receive the raw data from STDIN rather than a file path from a parameter.

Most of time we wont save the original input data. So we don't need to noise the user or interact with FS.

The content-type will be important and that can be informed by an env var, simpler then parse CLI parameters.

**Example:**
```bash
#!/data/data/com.termux/files/usr/bin/bash -e
# === This is a termux-file-editor script configuration header ===
# TERMUX_RECEIVE_FILE_BY=STDIN
# === That was a termux-file-editor script configuration header ===

if [ "$TERMUX_RECEIVE_FILE_MIME" = "text/plain" ]; then
  tot=$(wc -l)
  termux-toast -b blue "This text has $tot lines."
else
  termux-toast -b red "I don't know what to do."
fi
```

`TERMUX_RECEIVE_FILE_BY` could accept this values:
* `ASK`: The current approach, that opens a file save dialog **(default)**
* `TEMP`: Save to a temp file without user interaction. (Solves #1887 without breaking any script)
* `STDIN`: Push the file data as input without save any file. Most of times we wont save the original input data.

`TERMUX_RECEIVE_FILE_MIME` is a interesting information, useful for any receive method.

**Reference implementation**

_Does another app/terminal emulator have this feature?_
You can see the header configuration idea at:
* [Init scripts](https://refspecs.linuxbase.org/LSB_3.1.1/LSB-Core-generic/LSB-Core-generic/initscrcomconv.html)
* [User Scripts](https://wiki.greasespot.net/Metadata_Block)
title: Optionally send raw file data to termux-file-editor

author: aurium
body:
**Feature description**
Termux must save the shared file to a temp file and then give it to `~/bin/termux-file-editor`.
After `~/bin/termux-file-editor` exits, the temp file must be erased.

**Breaking change**
This change will not break most scripts. Scripts that need the file to be saved at an specific path will break.

**Why**
Today Termux asks the user to save the shared file before sending it to `~/bin/termux-file-editor`. It's not a necessary step and is not clear to the user why it is necessary. This must to be transparent to the non hacker user, and can be more direct to the hacker user.
title: Remove file save dialog before termux-file-editor call

author: richardb64
body:
**Feature description**
The terminal emulation of Termux needs the ability to configure the byte string generated by the "backspace" key on the keyboard (physical, soft, or "BKSP" in the extra-keys row of Termux itself), with keycode `KeyEvent.KEYCODE_DEL`.  Currently it seems to be hard-wired to send `^?` (ASCII DEL), with no way of changing it.  This might be the default expectation of many packages on Linux, but it is _not_ the expectation on many other systems, nor of the upstream packages (ncurses, xterm, etc) - Linux is the odd one out here.

An "advanced" option in `termux.properties` would be OK, though a GUI option would be better, and more in keeping with other terminal emulators.

Locally on the Android device within Termux, things are mostly consistent (eg. `stty erase` is set by default to `^?`).  However, when connecting remotely to other machines, often the remote machine expects `^H`, and there are usually reasons why this cannot be changed - notably not to break compatibility with other clients.  Therefore Termux needs to be able to send this character.

The Termux code (KeyHandler.java) attempts to send `^H` if the `Ctrl` key is used as a modifier with the Backspace key.  However, not all keyboards allow modifiers to be applied to this key (eg. "Hacker's Keyboard" doesn't), and in any case, having to use a modifier for the most common function would be a poor workaround.  The other workaround (to type `Ctrl-H` directly) is similarly a poor experience.

In addition to an explicit configuration option, it might also be worth supporting the "DEC Private Mode" codes - in this case, specifically `^[[?67h` and `^[[?67l` - as that would allow remote .bashrc scripts to adjust the emulation to match the expectations of the remote machine.

Similarly, the string sent by the "delete" key which appears on some keyboards with keycode `KeyEvent.KEYCODE_FORWARD_DEL` (and is available as "DEL" in Termux's extra-keys row) currently seems to be hard-wired to send the DEC/ANSI Keypad Delete sequence `^[[3~`.  That should be configurable too (eg. to send `^?`).

**Reference implementation**
Pretty much all terminal emulator programs allow this to be configured with a prominent GUI option - xterm, Gnome Terminal, PuTTY, etc.  Elsewhere in the Android world, ConnectBot also allows the action of the backspace key to be configured.
title: Terminal emulation: allow configuration of string sent by backspace key

author: ilyapashuk
body:
<!--
IMPORTANT:

1. Support of Android 5.x - 6.x is finished.
2. Fill the template AFTER comments.
-->

**Feature description**
<!--
Describe the feature and why you want it.
-->

Termux is most screen reader accessible terminal emulator that I seen.
In most popular android terminals screenreaders was even unable get a terminal view text, so that apps was fully unaccessible.
In termux, you provided a good accessibility because screenreaders are able to get view's text and notified about all it's changes.
but android screenreaders knows nothing about cli consoles and often handles sutch events incorrectly.
for exampl, on text change, they re reads full text, not only changed part, or reads nothing. Also, it's common practice that when some letter typed on the keyboard, physical or touch, screeners read that character and, when space are pressed, they read full word to make sure that it is correct, but in termux nothing of this works.

to make this app more accessible, you should add some changes to it's behavior:
view should not generate default TextChanged event, but it should use an AnnounceForAccessibility method to announce only new or changed textparts.
when a letter are typed, it should send that letter to AnnounceForAccessibility too.
when del key are typed, it should speak something like "%l deleted" where %l is deleted letter.
and on any cursor movement it should speak new cursor position (new cursored line in up-down movement and new word on left-right movement)

also, I want to test that changes before publication as an accessibility user, but I need a ready apk because at this time have not a working build instrumentation. And also it's version code should be equal with current release code because I have a lot of data in termux env and can't reinstall it to downgrade. The same sing for signing.
or it should be as different package name, failsafe mode will be enough for first time.

thank you
title: feature request: request for some accessibility impruvments

author: s20208413
body:
<!--
IMPORTANT:

1. Support of Android 5.x - 6.x is finished.
2. Fill the template AFTER comments.
-->

**Problem description**
<!--
A clear and concise description of what the problem is.
You may post screenshots in addition to description.
-->

Termux does not work on [MiReader](https://www.gearbest.com/blog/new-gear/xiaomi-mireader-e-book-review-an-excellent-amazon-kindle-alternative-9314) (MiReader is an eBook Reader by Xiaomi).

It just shows black screen:

![screenshot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sceext2/termux-app/screenshot/1-black.png)

**Steps to reproduce**
<!--
Steps to reproduce the behavior. Please post all necessary
commands that are needed to reproduce the issue.
-->

1. Install termux on MiReader.

2. Run termux (start [TermuxActivity](https://github.com/termux/termux-app/blob/v0.94/app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/TermuxActivity.java)).

**Expected behavior**
<!--
A clear and concise description of what you expected to happen.
-->

The command prompt should appear.

**Additional information**

* Termux application version:  `v0.94`
* Android OS version:  `Android 8.1`
* Device model:  MiReader  (system version `1.1.3.200115`)

**A workaround**

There is probably a BUG of the Android system on MiReader:  When start `TermuxActivity`, [`startService()`](https://github.com/termux/termux-app/blob/v0.94/app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/TermuxActivity.java#L318) in the [`onCreate()`](https://github.com/termux/termux-app/blob/v0.94/app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/TermuxActivity.java#L201) method does not work.  So [`TermuxService`](https://github.com/termux/termux-app/blob/v0.94/app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/TermuxService.java) will not start, and `mTermService` in the [`addNewSession()`](https://github.com/termux/termux-app/blob/v0.94/app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/TermuxActivity.java#L601) method is `null`.

Add [these lines of code](https://github.com/sceext2/termux-app/blob/sceext-debug/app/src/main/java/com/termux/app/TermuxActivity.java#L605) will solve this problem:

```
    void addNewSession(boolean failSafe, String sessionName) {
        if (mTermService == null) {
            Intent serviceIntent = new Intent(this, TermuxService.class);
            startService(serviceIntent);
            return;
        }
```

There will still be a black screen on the start, but slide from left of the screen and click the "NEW SESSION" button, then everything will be OK.

![screenshot-new-session](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sceext2/termux-app/screenshot/2-new-session.png)

![screenshot-ok](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sceext2/termux-app/screenshot/3-ok.png)

;-)
title: Termux does not work on MiReader

body:
<!-- Important note: Refusing to provide needed information may result in issue closing. If you are having problems with a package in termux then a bug report should be filed in the termux-packages repo: https://github.com/termux/termux-packages -->

**Problem description**

Termux magic keybindings in general seem to work on my Chromebook, but a few don't.

These key-bindings work:

- Ctl-Alt-V works to paste
- Ctl-Alt-C works to create a new session
- Ctl-Alt-R works to rename a session
- Ctl-Alt-N and Ctl-Alt-P work properly to switch between sessions
- Ctl-Alt-2 - Ctl-Alt-4 work properly for switching to the associated session (I didn't test beyond 4, but I'd guess 5-9 work as well)
- Ctl-Alt-M works to pop up the menu
- Ctl-Alt-+ and Ctl-Alt-- work to adjust the font size
- Ctl-Alt-ΓåÉ and Ctl-Alt-ΓåÆ work to open/close the side menu
- Ctl-Alt-U works to pop up a URL menu

However, the following key-bindings *don't* seem to work properly:

- Ctl-Alt-1 does not work to switch to session 1 (odd, as the other numbered keys do work)
- Ctl-Alt-Γåæ and Ctl-Alt-Γåô do not work to switch between sessions
- Ctl-Alt-F does not work to toggle full-screen (however the display/windowing on ChromeOS is sufficiently different from Android that I'm not sure I actually expected this to work)

These non-working keys seem to simply do nothing.


**Steps to reproduce**

1. Create multiple sessions in Termux on a Chromebook.

3. Switch to session 2 (e.g.)

author: snogglethorpe
body:
<!-- Important note: Refusing to provide needed information may result in issue closing. If you are having problems with a package in termux then a bug report should be filed in the termux-packages repo: https://github.com/termux/termux-packages -->

**Problem description**

I'm using Termux on ChromeOS (with Android support), on a Samsung Chromebook Pro, on the Beta channel (ChromeOS version 78.0.3904.70).

When Termux is in "full screen mode" -- no title bar, uses entire screen, as enabled by the dedicated "full screen" key on the keyboard -- there's a big chunk of wasted space at the bottom of the Termux window, about 2cm high, which is entirely black.

This isn't the "extra keys" display; I've disabled that via the side-swipe menu.  If I enable that, the row of extra key buttons is shown _above_ the blank chuck at the bottom of the window.

Increasing or decreasing the font size via two-finger pinching does not change this; the font size correctly changes, and the terminal redisplays, but the unused black chunk remains at the bottom.

I've attached a screenshot; this is pretty basic, just notice that at the bottom there's a big black area (this isn't the Emacs echo/message area, which is only a single line high).

If I _don't_ use the "full screen" mode (so that a title bar is shown at the top of the Termux window), even if I drag the window boundaries so that the window occupies the entire display, the wasted black space goes away, and Termux correctly uses all space down to the bottom of its window.  However, in this case there's of course space wasted due to the titlebar at the top of the Termux window.... ><

I notice that the wasted space is roughly the same as the size of the ChromeOS shelf, so my guess is that Termux is somehow trying to avoid conflicting with the shelf ... however as I have shelf auto-hiding turned on (not sure whether this is the default), the shelf is not displayed most of the time, so Termux really shouldn't be worrying about it.

Thanks!

![Screenshot 2019-10-27 at 12 23 09](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/398170/67640035-a787b000-f8b4-11e9-932d-73a95112487e.png)


**Steps to reproduce**

1. Use a ChromeOS system (e.g. a Chromebook) with Android support.
2. Install Termux from the Play Store.
3. Make sure ChromeOS shelf auto-hiding is enabled.  From the ChromeOS help text:  "To configure the Shelf to hide itself automatically, right-click or ALT-click on the Shelf. From the menu that appears, click on the Autohide Shelf option."
4. Start Termux.
5. Make sure Termux is in "full screen mode" (no title bar) by using the dedicated Chromebook key.
6. Do something that allows you to see how much of the terminal window is actually used by Termux (e.g. just type "termux-info" to show a bunch of text).
7. Notice that there's a black area about 2cm high at the bottom which is never used.


**Expected behavior**

I expected Termux to use its entire window to display text.


**Additional information**
Post output of command `termux-info`.
If you are rooted or have access to adb then capture a logcat with `logcat -d "*:W"`, from a adb or root shell.

):
```
Q03 Why doesnΓÇÖt Multi-Window work, even though android:resizeableActivity="true" has been declared in the Manifest?

Your app might have targeting the wrong targetSDKVersion. Android:resizeableActivity is supported when the targetSDK version is greater than or equal to 24 (Android N). IF targetSDKVesion is under 24, activities are only resizable when the screenOrientation is not set as fixed. Check to make sure you do not have this code in your manifest:

<activity
android:screenOrientation="portrait | landscape">
</activity>
title: Samsung Dex support - multiple windows

author: easyaspi314
body:
<!-- Important note: Refusing to provide needed information may result in issue closing. If you are having problems with a package in termux then a bug report should be filed in the termux-packages repo: https://github.com/termux/termux-packages -->

**Problem description**
Long lines which are overwritten with `'\r'` are very buggy, causing issues with wrapping.

**Steps to reproduce**

1. Compile this C program:
```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(int argc, char *argv[])
{
    if (argc != 2) {
       printf("usage: %s cols\n", argv[0]);
       return 1;
    }

    int cols = atoi(argv[1]);
    if (cols < 3) return 1;

    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < 3; j++) {
            putchar('0' + i);
            putchar(' ');
            for (int k = 0; k < cols - 2; k++) {
                putchar('A' + (k % 26));
            }
            putchar('\r');
       }
       putchar('\n');
    }
}
```
```
$ cd
$ termux-clipboard-get > cols.c
$ clang cols.c -o cols
```
2. Run the following commands:
```
$ ./cols $(( $COLUMNS ))
$ ./cols $(( $COLUMNS + 1 ))
```
3. Zoom, block copy, etc and more.


**Expected behavior**
Given a 10 column Termux, we should see:
```
$ ./cols 10
0 ABCDEFGH
1 ABCDEFGH
2 ABCDEFGH
$ ./cols 11
0 ABCDEFGH
I
1 ABCDEFGH
I
2 ABCDEFGH
I
```
What actually happens:
```
0 ABCDEFGH
0 ABCDEFGH
0 ABCDEFGH
I
1 ABCDEFGH
1 ABCDEFGH
1 ABCDEFGH
I
2 ABCDEFGH
2 ABCDEFGH
2 ABCDEFGH
I
```

Zooming also breaks columns (it staggers), and copying often replaces newlines with excess spaces.
title: Long lines with \r are not handled properly

author: maxigaz
body:
**Feature description**
I use the python program called termdown (which can be installed via `pip install termdown`) as a timer by letting it run in a session while I'm working in another one.

The problem is that as soon as I switch to another session, a heads-up notification keeps displaying the current status of the timer. This can make work quite difficult because it covers some of the screen elements and distracts me by constantly showing the currently remaining time.

Here's an example screenshot when `termdown 10m` is running in a background session:

![Screenshot_20190528-111316_termux](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10419911/58554755-1a163600-8218-11e9-8233-c6dd51e585ff.png)

Is it possible to make this heads-up notification optional or configurable, at least in this case?

**Reference implementation**
Konsole and Yakuake (together with KDE Plasma) normally show a notification only when termdown has ended. (This behaviour is fully configurable under NotificationsΓÇª ΓåÆ Activity detected.) I think if Termux did the same, it would be much more convenient.
title: Option to disable/customise heads-up notification for background sessions

author: FlyingWombat
body:
I'm a little surprised I haven't found anything regarding this, so please point it out if I missed it.  
Also, I know very little about android development, so please tell me if there is a reason this isn't feasible.   
I've read about "Text Input View" mentioned on this [wiki page](https://wiki.termux.com/wiki/Touch_Keyboard), but it's not as useful for the shell as it is for text editors.

As the title says, I think a great UX improvement would be autocomplete/suggestions/autocorrect when entering commands in the shell with the virtual keyboard.

I'll admit, I don't really know how this would be implemented.  
Since most shells (bash, zsh, fish, etc.) already have fairly advanced completion sources, one idea would be to leverage these sources -- along with info collected from shell history -- and feed it to the virtual keyboard's completion engine. This would, of course, require Android's virtual keyboard to have a standard protocol for modifying the completion dictionary.

The benefit of this would be much more natural input to the shell; as we are all used to using the keyboard's suggestion bar to help us type; as opposed to hitting the tab button a bunch of times.
title: [Feature Request] Keyboard autocomplete suggestions

author: robertvandeneynde
body:
On macOS X, the `pbcopy` and `pbpaste` command can be used to manipulate the clipboard.

On Desktop linux that's the `xclip -selection` command, so I made `alias pbcopy='xclip -selection clipboard'` and `alias pbpaste='xclip -selection clipboard -o'` for the command to be portable with macOS X.

That's very useful for a lot of text replacement applications :

```bash
pbpaste > file.txt     # into a file
pbcopy file.txt        # from a file
cat file.txt | pbcopy  # from a file, using a pipe
pbpaste | sed 's/ /-/g' | pbcopy  # direct replacement of clipboard
pbpaste | python 'import sys
sys.stdout.write(sys.stdin.read().replace(" ", "-"))
' | pbcopy  # use python for more complicated Operations !
pbpaste | python replace_stuff.py | pbcopy  # why not from a python file ?
```

I have no clue how clipboard work in android but I think that's manageable !
title: termux-clipboard is not so easy to install (in order to support copy paste via pbcopy, pbpaste commands)

author: mklein994
body:
It would be great if support could be added for styled underlines.

Here's a brief overview:
- `\e[4:1m` straight underline
- `\e[4:2m` double underline
- `\e[4:3m` curly underline

**Note**: the colon is strictly required, as something like `\e[4;1mHello\e[0m` would print a bold, underlined "Hello" to the console. This is discussed in the [neovim issue](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/7479#issuecomment-348783792).

Support for this was added to neovim in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/7479, and in tmux (so far without color support) in https://github.com/tmux/tmux/issues/1492. There's an idea floating around that the underline can be given a color using `58:2:<r>:<g>:<b>` or `58:5:<x11 color>`, similar to how it's done for true colors. From what I've read, `CSI 59` resets the underline color. (I'm not a terminal developer, so please forgive me if I'm butchering these terms).

Here's an example:
```bash
echo -e '\e[4:3;58:2:255:100:0mHello\e[0m'
```
![undercurl](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3069239/46876270-836d4400-ce03-11e8-9a12-1ad5bb9a7f9b.PNG)
This produces "Hello", with an orange wavy underline. I've tested it and it works on both [mintty](https://github.com/mintty/mintty/wiki/Tips#text-attributes-and-rendering) and [kitty](https://sw.kovidgoyal.net/kitty/protocol-extensions.html#colored-and-styled-underlines).
title: Support styled underlines

author: hyperpallium
body:
This is really useful in `vim`, when you turn off line wrapping (`:set nowrap`) to get more lines per screen (or, use a larger font), and you can easily scroll to see the few lines that extend off the right of the screen.

termux already converts up/down swipes into mouse wheel events, so you can scroll. In `vim`, these can be mapped like:

```
  map <ScrollWheelUp> <C-Y>
  map <ScrollWheelDown> <C-E>
```

But `vim` can also accept horizontal mouse wheel events:

```
  map <ScrollWheelLeft> z<Left>
  map <ScrollWheelRight> z<Right>
```

But termux doesn't seem to be generating them.

This seems like it would be simple to add, and it wouldn't interfere with the swipe from the left to bring up the window list and keyboard/new session, because that starts from the edge of the display.
title: Request: horizontal mouse wheel (from left/right touch swipes)

author: robolange
body:
I use Termux (latest available in Play Store) on ChromeOS (66.0.3359.203 on an Asus C301S) and run a number of console programs that can accept terminal mouse input (emacs, weechat, tmux). When a console program accepts mouse input, Termux passes the mouse events through and it works nicely, but at the same time, it disables the long-press to open the selection menu. Thus, my choice is to use terminal mouse events XOR to be able to copy text from Termux to ChromeOS. (Note: paste from ChromeOS to Termux is unaffected, b/c the hardware keyboard Ctrl+Alt+V allows it to work.)

I have currently handled this by explicitly disabling terminal mouse events in my Termux dotfiles. However, this is suboptimal because it represents a (currently the only) difference between my regular Linux dotfiles and Termux dotfiles, and using the mouse in console programs is occasionally useful. If I can get a way of using terminal mouse events AND copying text from Termux to ChromeOS, then I can use a single set of dotfiles for both environments.

I'm thinking of two alternative ways to solve this:

1. A new hardware keyboard shortcut to activate the selection menu. This would probably be the easiest, but I'd rather avoid making more Termux shortcuts that might conflict with peoples' existing keybindings.

2. Provide a way to switch from the regular popup menu (activated by keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Alt+M) to the selection menu. You can currently switch from the selection menu to the regular popup menu by clicking the "More..." button; perhaps there could be a "Selection..." button to switch from the regular popup menu to the selection menu?

Another alternative that I'm guessing would be more complex to implement:

3. Gnome Terminal has a similar issue that terminal apps accepting mouse events disables its built-in mouse actions. With Gnome Terminal, when you hold the Shift key, mouse events are handled directly by Gnome Terminal and not passed through to the app running in the terminal.

Any thoughts or ideas?
title: chromeos: better support for using terminal mouse events AND selection/copy?

author: fat-tire
body:
So I modded the [Termux-Styling](https://github.com/termux/termux-styling) app to support android's new [downloadable font](https://developer.android.com/guide/topics/ui/look-and-feel/downloadable-fonts) feature, but I ran into two issues that need resolving:

*  Downloadable fonts requires a valid font provider.  The most popular one is Google Play, but that means that Google Play is required to be installed to get access to them.  No big deal, can fallback to the current bundled fonts if it's not installed.

*  The thing is though, when you request one of the downloadable fonts, you're handed a [TypeFace](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/graphics/Typeface) object, NOT a .ttf file, which is what the Styling app currently needs to save for the Termux app to use.   You can easily [go from a font file to a TypeFace object](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/graphics/Typeface.html#createFromFile(java.io.File)), but not vice versa.  So while I can currently get the Typeface in the styling app, there's not much I can do with it.

There is another option, which is to use Google's font API to get the actual URI to the .ttf file for the font, and then use the Termux Styling app to download that file and copy it into the right place.  This wouldn't require modifying anything in Termux app, but it would require entering a ([freely available](https://developers.google.com/fonts/docs/developer_api#APIKey)) API key to be able to access that and that might be too annoying to add this.  Might also as a FOURTH option make a list of all the .ttf paths but that may change in the future.

The question-- would it make more sense to just add the downloadable font support to the termux app itself?  It would barely add any size to the app footprint (in fact could make it smaller if you're not loading all those unused fonts in Styling) and fonts are cached by Google Play and reused between apps.  Downside would be that (1) this doesn't affect color stuff, (2) it does have the Google Play dependency and so wouldn't add much for FDroid users, and (3) I am unclear as to whether then you move the color support into termux app or keep the styling or what.

Just throwing it out there if anyone wants to offer any thoughts or opinions.
title: Add hundreds of downloadable fonts to termux (discussion)

author: konijn
body:
Greetings,

it seems according to
http://www.unicode.org/Public/6.1.0/ucd/StandardizedVariants.html
that a number of unicode characters have 2 variations (text style and emoji style).

And Termux renders (at least for the suit characters ΓÖá ΓÖÑ ΓÖª ΓÖú) in emoji style which is wrong for a terminal, especially when those characters appears in a curses app. The emoji style will actually override the color assigned by the curses app.

To reproduce, just copy paste the following into the terminal: ΓÖá ΓÖÑ ΓÖª ΓÖú
This does not happen in the Cygwin Shell or the Tera Terminal.
title: Console renders standard variants as emoji's

author: robsmith11
body:
Ok finally figured out why mosh was sometimes having issues from within my chroot in termux: when termux is first opened, it will report the window size as 0 rows and 0 columns, but if the screen is rotated while termux is open, it will then report the correct rows and columns from that point forward.

This python script can be used to test this:
```
import sys, struct, fcntl, termios
s = struct.pack('HHHH', 0, 0, 0, 0)
t = fcntl.ioctl(sys.stdout.fileno(), termios.TIOCGWINSZ, s)
print(struct.unpack('HHHH', t))
```

Before the screen is rotated, it will output `(0,0,0,0)`, but after it's rotated, it will output something like `(26,96,0,0)`.
title: TIOCGWINSZ reports zero window rows/cols until screen is rotated

author: Keith-S-Thompson
body:
I use the "Black on white" color scheme, which displays text without colors.

When programs attempt to change the background color, the text should still be legible when using the "Black on white" or "White on black" scheme, but in fact both the foreground and the background are often black.

This script illustrates the problem:

```
#!/bin/bash

echo $'Black   \e[40mbackground\e[m'
echo $'Red     \e[41mbackground\e[m'
echo $'Green   \e[42mbackground\e[m'
echo $'Yellow  \e[43mbackground\e[m'
echo $'Blue    \e[44mbackground\e[m'
echo $'Magenta \e[45mbackground\e[m'
echo $'Cyan    \e[46mbackground\e[m'
echo $'White   \e[47mbackground\e[m'
```
Screenshots of the resulting output (on my Moto G5+ running Android 7.0) are here:
https://imgur.com/gallery/1DlEb, showing the output with the "Solarized Dark", "Black on white", "White on black", and "Solarized Light" color themes.

I first noticed this problem running `tmux` under Termux. The status line at the bottom of the screen is just a black bar with the "Black on white" theme. (There should be a way to work around this in `tmux`. I'm still trying to find it, but that's not the subject of this bug report.)
title: Illegible background using "Black on white" or "White on black" themes

author: lars18th
body:
Hi,

I recently started to use Termux. And I find it very interesting. For years I have used other Linux-on-Android systems; but I think this project is superior: simple and effective.

After this short intro I propose a new idea around the ΓÇ£suΓÇ¥ and ΓÇ£sudoΓÇ¥ commands. Please, note that this isnΓÇÖt a request of ΓÇ£how to use root in TermuxΓÇ¥ or ΓÇ£scripts for sudoΓÇ¥. I know about such tools, and this is a _different_ proposal:

- The problem: If you grant the root permission to Termux, then _any_ script running inside it can obtain root privileges (very bad!).

- The solution: Create a new Termux Add-on, called Termux:Sudo. This addon will be another _different_ Android App. This new application will have an structure similar to Termux:API, so it can communicate with the Termux shell environment. But this new app focuses on running processes with elevated privileges. In consequence, this Termux:Sudo is the _only_ app that needs to receive the root privileges.

- The implementation: The new Termux:Sudo will maintain an internal password for root (obfuscated) and a list of grant commands (or ΓÇÿ*ΓÇÖ for all). So the function is very similar to the ΓÇ£sudoΓÇ¥ command, and configured in the same way. Note that the data of Termux:Sudo App is _inaccessible_ from the Termux environment, so the password and configuration is even secure.

- The use: When you call for example ΓÇ£sudo nano /system/build.propΓÇ¥ a password command is presented prior to execute the binary.

What you think about this idea?
title: Termux:Sudo Addon proposal

Firstly, thanks so much for making such an awesome app!  I love termux.

I'm trying to launch an external activity from within termux while giving it the ability to write to a file (or, if possible, a FIFO).

It would appear that TermuxOpenReceiver can almost handle my needs, except that it is currently only designed to handle read-only access.  Having the ability to edit files from other apps would be very useful for me.

I would therefore kindly request that TermuxOpenReceiver handle `ACTION_EDIT`, in which case it would add the flag `Intent.FLAG_GRANT_WRITE_URI_PERMISSION` as well as open the file descriptor using `ParcelFileDescriptor.MODE_READ_WRITE`. Finally, it is currently restricted to files only due to an `isFile()` check.  Could it be changed to `exists()` instead?  I'm hoping that I could get it to work with a named pipe rather than a regular file so that I could pipe data from the activity back to the terminal.

author: max-mapper
body:
Hi, I am trying to use Termux to share files between my android and laptop, but I can't get any code that uses multicast DNS to work. I believe it is because Termux needs to request a MulticastLock: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/13221736/android-device-not-receiving-multicast-package/13223018#13223018

To test if it's working, do `pkg install node` and then `npm install multicast-dns` and then run `node example.js` with this example: https://github.com/mafintosh/multicast-dns/blob/master/example.js. You should see multicast traffic from your network, including your own query.
title: Support multicast DNS (mdns) via MulticastLock

author: crozone
body:
The Blackberry PRIV and KEYone have gesture capable physical keyboards that allow the moving of the text cursor in standard text inputs, in a mode known as "fine cursor control". It would be great to translate these smoothly into arrow key inputs, to allow fast bash history scrollback, and cursor navigation in programs like vim.

Currently, the "Up" and "Down" gestures already trigger the up and down arrow keys in Termux, but not in a smooth or predictable manner. The left and right gestures currently do nothing.

It's likely that this support would also cover software keyboards that allow cursor movement through built in controls and gestures.

The Blackberry Captivate Keyboard Integration Guide is [here](https://community.blackberry.com/docs/DOC-10328), however it mainly relates to using the keyboard as a trackpad/scroll wheel, which isn't the case when the keyboard is in text input mode (like it always is in termux). Likely the messages it is sending the application in cursor navigation mode are higher level and unrelated.
title: Convert text navigation gestures into arrow key inputs

author: kaushalmodi
body:
Hello,

I realized today that the time zone used by emacs to show the time internally was incorrect. i.e. `date` in Termux shows the correct time in correct zone. But time shown within emacs in Termux was BST time zone.

I started this conversation by mistake in the wrong thread [here][1].

Basically emacs relies on the `TZ` environment variable value to show the time correctly -- [[Source][2]].

Copying @Neo-Oli as he helped me confirm this issue. He uses `export TZ=$(getprop persist.sys.timezone)` in his `~/.bash_profile` to get around this issue.

Can this (the `TZ` variable) be set by default?

Thanks.

[1]: https://github.com/termux/termux-packages/issues/1076#issuecomment-328051119
[2]: https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Time-Zone-Rules.html
title: Auto-set TZ environment variable

author: redyoshi49q
body:
When running a newly created `screen` session with vertically and/or horizontally split panes, the touch screen can be used to select a pane within `screen`, but many touch screen interactions with command line programs (notably scrolling) do not function for programs such as `less` and `man` that are run inside of `screen` windows, even though such interactions work as expected when tested outside of `screen` (`emacs` is a notable exception; both cursor placement and scrolling function as expected for an `emacs` instance that's running inside of a `screen` window).

However, if a `screen` session is detached and then reattached, the touch screen interacts differently for both `screen` and the programs that are run inside of it. `screen` panes can no longer be selected with the touch screen (requiring the use of the C-a Tab key combo instead), but programs that are run within `screen` windows (`less`, `man`, etc.) appear to interact with the touch screen in the same way that they would if they were run outside of a `screen` session.  An exception to this generalization is the `bash` shell itself; while `bash` doesn't accept input from the touch screen when run outside of `screen`, a `bash` instance that is running inside of a reattached `screen` session seems to process movement across the touch screen as if presses of the arrow keys were being made.

I imagine that it might not be technically feasible to allow the touch screen's input to reliably affect both `screen` (through selecting window panes) and apps running inside of `screen` (through scrolling and other interactions) at the same time.  In any case, it may be desirable to adjust the behavior of `screen` in Termux such that the behavior of newly spawned `screen` sessions and newly attached `screen` sessions is the same in order to create a more consistent user experience.

In case it's of relevance, I quote my Termux environment's .inputrc below.  I have a few other environment files (including a .bashrc and .screenrc), but no others seem potentially related to this issue; let me know if you'd like to see any other files.

	$include /etc/inputrc

	"\e[A": history-search-backward
	"\e[B": history-search-forward
	"\e[C": forward-char
	"\e[D": backward-char
	"\eOC": forward-word
	"\eOD": backward-word
	"\C-H": kill-whole-line
	"\C-_": kill-whole-line

	C-p: history-search-backward
	C-n: history-search-forward
	#C-p: previous-history
	#C-n: next-history
	C-f: forward-word
	C-b: backward-word
	C-k: kill-line
	C-y: yank
	#C-_: undo // apparently, C-Backspace maps to this in mintty(?)

	$if Bash
		Space: magic-space
	$endif

	#set horizontal-scroll-mode on

On a final note, I greatly appreciate the implementation of `screen` in Termux; I had sorely missed the utility and familiarity of `screen` previous to that.
title: Inconsistent touch response behavior within multiple pane screen session

author: BrainDamage
body:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sixel is a raster protocol format part of VT200 and VT300 serial terminals
it recently started gaining traction on command line tools to output images directly to the terminal because it works trought ssh, works reasonably well, etc 
for an implementation detail to copy the encoder/decoder, one can look at https://github.com/saitoha/libsixel , the url documents also a series of terminals and programs that support the standard
I realize it's a low priority feature, but it'd allow rather nice semi graphical workflow under termux's terminal, currently  it's already possible to view sixel graphics from launching a ssh daemon and connecting externally with a sixel capable terminal emulator
title: Feature Request: sixel graphics mode

body:
# Problem

When inputting Japanese characters (Kanji, Hiragana, Katakana), we usually toggle Japanese-input mode by halfwidth/fullwidth (σìèΦºÆ/σà¿ΦºÆ) key ([see wikipedia for details](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Language_input_keys)).

author: mozzwald
body:
The terminal bell works when the app is running in the foreground with the screen on but after screen turns off or the app is running in the background it no longer plays a sound or vibrates based on the _bell-character_ setting in _termux.properties_.

This option is useful for being notified of events if connected to another machine via ssh (where termux-notification will not work). Please add support for terminal bell as a notification.

author: torappinfo
body:
Since console apps has no knowledge of gestures, if we can convert pageUp/Down gestures to keys or mouse wheel events, it will enable more creative usages in termux.

For example, current gesture support is non-functional when using less in neovim terminal mode.

to reproduce the bug, enter neovim terminal mode with:
$ nvim -c term
Inside the neovim terminal mode, run 
$ less txtfile
Then pageUp/Down gestures trigger neovim quit terminal insert mode and thus make it nonfunctional.

Running less in neovim terminal mode in linux, pressing pageUp/Down keys or mouse wheel works perfectly.
title: pageUp/Down gesture supported as pageUp/Down keys (or mouse wheel)

author: torappinfo
body:
In android soft keyboards, sometimes there will be some predicted inputs (usually underlined) in the text area waiting for confirmation. Termux can not take these inputs even after confirmation.

To reproduce, use a soft keyboard for input in search bar, find an input mode (usually handwriting mode will do), where some underlined predicted text will be firstly shown in search bar before confirmation. After further inputs, these underlined text will become solid (not underlined again). Termux has issues to take these underlined inputs.

One specific example is Shouxin input method for CJK in handwriting mode. Termux simply does not take any underlined candidates.
title: termux cannot take the underlined candidates with soft keyboards

author: vaites
body: When any app or script asks for a password the keyboard doesn't change, but in Android the password fields uses an [input method type](http://developer.android.com/intl/es/training/keyboard-input/style.html). Is possible to detect this behavior and change keyboard type?
title: Change keyboard type when asking for a password

body:

First of all, thanks for the excellent work; combined with Blackberry Priv's in-built keyboard, this makes editing stuff on the go a breeze... except a minor problem: the Alt key on the keyboard (or Meta) sends escape instead of special symbols.

i.e.,

0033 27 0x1B Escape '^[' or '\e'

This looks very much like `metaSendsEscape` option turned on inside xterm. I suppose there is very simple way to make it work otherwise, any pointers?

author: Neo-Oli
body:
In certain programs you can use mouse gestures to do various tasks, for example:
- tmux lets you resize panes with click and drag.
- weechat has swipe left and right gestures to switch buffer. 

This works when connecting to termux via SSH from a computer with mouse but it does not work when using the termux app. 
I realize that this would cause a conflict with the swipe to scroll feature, but maybe we can come up with a solution that can incorporate both these features. 
Some ways I came up with: 
- Move the long press menu into the left side drawer and have long press function as a click and drag. 
- Have a switch (or one time function like select) in the long press menu to switch between click and drag and scroll. In the two programs I came across with mouse dragging it is not something you have to do all the time, so having an additional step between might not be that bad. 

Maybe someone has a better idea?
title: Add support for mouse gestures.
